## 122. The Scoring Cliff at Scale (Full Benchmark)

From the C23 full benchmark, the criteria-to-pass conversion remains brutal:

| Criteria Rate Band | Categories | Avg Pass Rate |
|-------------------|-----------|---------------|
| 86-89% | 4 | 32.4% |
| 83-85% | 12 | 14.5% |
| 77-81% | 8 | 11.2% |

Even the BEST criteria band (86-89%) only converts to 32.4% task pass rate. This is the scoring cliff (§86) at work across 1,251 tasks.

### The Tightest Category

data-privacy-cybersecurity achieves 88.9% criteria but only 22.7% pass — a massive disconnect. This category likely has tasks with 55+ criteria, where 88.9% per-criterion still predicts ~0.89^55 ≈ 0.10% all-pass probability. The actual 22.7% means criteria failures are clustered (not random), which is exploitable.

---

## 123. Definitive Per-Verb Performance (C23, 1,251 Tasks)

The full Harvey LAB benchmark has 30 distinct task verbs. Performance clusters into 4 tiers:

### Tier 1: Composition Verbs (>25% pass)

| Verb | Tasks | Pass | Rate | Criteria% |
|------|-------|------|------|-----------|
| draft | 474 | 165 | **34.8%** | 90.4% |
| analyze | 97 | 29 | **29.9%** | 85.4% |

These two verbs represent 571/1,251 tasks (45.6%) and 194/213 passes (91.1%). The system is fundamentally a composition engine. Draft tasks produce 90.4% criteria — the highest of any high-volume verb.

### Tier 2: Review/Triage (10-15% pass)

| Verb | Tasks | Pass | Rate | Criteria% |
|------|-------|------|------|-----------|
| triage | 8 | 1 | 12.5% | 84.9% |
| review | 82 | 9 | 11.0% | 81.2% |

Review tasks require systematic evaluation, not composition. The system handles ~11% but misses completeness requirements.

### Tier 3: Extraction/Comparison (1-5% pass)

| Verb | Tasks | Pass | Rate | Criteria% |
|------|-------|------|------|-----------|
| extract | 150 | 8 | 5.3% | 75.1% |
| compare | 152 | 2 | **1.3%** | 76.7% |
| identify | 211 | 2 | **0.9%** | 74.4% |

These three verbs represent 513/1,251 tasks (41.0%) but only 12/213 passes (5.6%). The system fails at precision enumeration. With 211 tasks, identify is the LARGEST verb and passes at 0.9%.

### Tier 4: Dead Verbs (0% pass, >4 tasks)

| Verb | Tasks | Pass | Rate | Criteria% |
|------|-------|------|------|-----------|
| research | 10 | 0 | 0% | 83.4% |
| summarize | 8 | 0 | 0% | 82.7% |
| build | 5 | 0 | 0% | 89.8% |
| map | 5 | 0 | 0% | **92.5%** |

`map` achieves 92.5% criteria (highest of ANY verb) but 0% task pass. These 5 tasks are likely all-or-nothing: map out a complete structure where one missing element = failure.

### The Verb Tax

| Scenario | Est. Passes | Est. Rate |
|----------|-------------|-----------|
| Current verb distribution | 213 | 17.0% |
| If all tasks were draft | ~435 | 34.8% |
| If identify→draft conversion | 213 + ~73 | 22.9% |
| If identify+extract+compare→draft | 213 + ~179 | 31.3% |

Converting just identify tasks to draft-verb behavior would add ~73 passes. Converting all three Tier 3 verbs would nearly double the pass count.

### Implications for Architecture

1. **Verb-specific worker prompts** (§69) are the highest-leverage extraction change. Identify tasks need enumeration behavior: "list ALL items, return when you've checked every section."

2. **Compare tasks** (152 tasks, 1.3%) are structurally different from extract/identify. They require cross-document alignment: reading two versions and systematically matching provisions. The current worker dispatch doesn't support paired-document workers.

3. **The 474 draft tasks at 34.8%** are the easiest wins. If synthesis improvements (lower SECTION_THRESHOLD, larger output budgets) push draft criteria from 90.4% to 95%, binary scoring math predicts pass rate could reach 50-60%.

---

## 124. Anomalous Verbs: High Criteria, Zero Passes

Two verbs achieve >85% criteria but 0% passes:

| Verb | Tasks | Criteria% | Why 0%? |
|------|-------|-----------|---------|
| build | 5 | 89.8% | Timeline construction — missing temporal sequencing |
| map | 5 | 92.5% | Complete structure mapping — binary completeness requirement |

These 10 tasks are near-misses where targeted fixes would have outsized impact. A 92.5% criteria rate with 0% passes means the failing criteria are universally missed across all 5 tasks — a systematic gap, not stochastic.

### categorize: The Unicorn

One `categorize` task achieves 98.5% criteria (1 criterion short of passing). This is the closest to passing of any failing task in the entire benchmark. Finding and fixing that single criterion would add 1 pass immediately.

---

## 125. Plan Coverage Review: Detection Works, Repair Fails 50% (C27)

Examined 13 tasks' plan_coverage_review.json and plan_coverage_repair.json:

### The Paradox: Passing Tasks Have Empty Reviews

| Task Group | Review Items | Gaps Found | Repair Success |
|-----------|-------------|------------|----------------|
| 5 passing tasks | 0 seed, 0 criteria | 0 | N/A |
| 8 failing tasks | 8-10 seed, 6-8 criteria | 4-16 per task | **50%** |

Passing tasks have COMPLETELY EMPTY plan coverage reviews — zero seed questions checked, zero criteria checked, zero gaps. The system treats absence of analysis as success.

Failing tasks have RICH reviews: 8-10 seed questions evaluated, 6-8 criteria checked, with 4-16 gaps identified per task. The review module CORRECTLY identifies what's missing. But repair fails.

### Repair Parse Error Rate: 50%

| Repair Outcome | Tasks | Repairs Made |
|---------------|-------|--------------|
| Successful (parse_error=false) | 4 | 5-7 gaps each |
| Failed (parse_error=true) | 4 | 0 gaps each |

4 of 8 failing tasks have `parse_error: TRUE` in plan_coverage_repair.json, meaning the repair model's output couldn't be parsed. These tasks get ZERO repairs despite having identified gaps.

### Gap Work Types and Repairability

| Work Type | Occurrences | Repairable? |
|-----------|------------|-------------|
| extract_more | 32 | Sometimes (but excluded from state repair §64) |
| compare | 6 | Sometimes |
| calculate | 2 | Sometimes |
| recommendation | 1 | Yes |
| unanswerable | 6 | Never |
| legal_authority | 1 | Never |

The most common gap type (extract_more, 32 occurrences) is explicitly EXCLUDED from `_select_repair_gaps()` in state_conversion.py (§64). So even when detected and non-parsed, these gaps are architecturally unfixable.

### Implication: The Repair Pipeline Is the Weakest Post-Loop Link

1. **Review correctly identifies gaps** (detection works)
2. **Repair model fails to parse output 50% of the time** (execution fails)
3. **Most common gap type (extract_more) is excluded from repair** (architecture blocks fix)
4. **Passing tasks bypass review entirely** (no validation of "good enough")

---

## 126. The State Conversion Parse Error Rate Across Tasks (C27)

From the state_conversion_review.json files:

| Result | Tasks | Percentage |
|--------|-------|-----------|
| parse_error: false | 5 (passing) | 38% |
| parse_error: true | 8 (failing) | 62% |

The 56.3% state conversion parse error rate (§65) is confirmed at 62% in this sample. But the correlation with pass/fail may be coincidental — state conversion runs AFTER the swarm loop and before synthesis. Its failure means observations don't get upgraded to analysis entries, but the raw observations still flow to curation.

### The 62% Parse Error Is a Model Output Quality Issue

State conversion asks the model to convert observations into structured analysis entries. The model sometimes returns malformed JSON. This is NOT a code bug — it's a prompt/model reliability issue. The fix is:
1. Retry with a simpler prompt structure
2. Use schema-constrained generation
3. Fall back to raw observations (current behavior, but silently)

---

## 127. Single-File vs Multi-File: The 10.5x Gap at Full Scale (C23, 1,251 Tasks)

The most important structural finding in the entire analysis, now confirmed at full benchmark scale:

| Output Files | Tasks | Pass | Rate | Ratio |
|-------------|-------|------|------|-------|
| 1 file | 964 | 56 | **5.8%** | 1.0x |
| 2 files | 254 | 155 | **61.0%** | **10.5x** |
| 3+ files | 33 | 11 | 33.3% | 5.7x |

### The Numbers

- **77.1% of all tasks** (964/1,251) produce a single output file and pass at only 5.8%
- **20.3% of all tasks** (254/1,251) produce 2 output files and pass at 61.0%
- **72.8% of all passes** (155/213) come from 2-file tasks

### Why 3+ Files Drops to 33.3%

Tasks with 3+ output files are more complex (they require spreading content across more deliverables). The item assignment becomes harder — must_include items get split thinner across more files, potentially pushing some below SECTION_THRESHOLD.

### The Output Format Distribution

| Format | Files | Percentage |
|--------|-------|-----------|
| .docx | 1,613 | 97.5% |
| .xlsx | 41 | 2.5% |
| .md | 1 | <0.1% |

The benchmark is almost entirely DOCX-based. The 41 XLSX files likely correspond to spreadsheet-oriented tasks (financial modeling, extraction tables). The single .md file is an anomaly.

### The Structural Fix Is Clear

If ALL 964 single-file tasks were routed through file-scoped synthesis (with a mandatory companion memo, or with SECTION_THRESHOLD lowered to 15), pass rate would increase dramatically:
- Current: 213/1,251 = 17.0%
- If single-file tasks matched 2-file rate (61%): 213 + (964 × 0.61 - 56) = **745/1,251 = 59.6%**
- Even at half the 2-file rate (30%): 213 + (964 × 0.30 - 56) = **446/1,251 = 35.7%**

This is the single highest-leverage structural change available.

---

## 128. The Overlooked Fix: Force Multi-File Output for Single-File Tasks

The analysis consistently points to one conclusion: the synthesis pipeline's dual codepath (monolithic vs file-scoped) is the root cause of 77% of failures.

### Evidence Summary

| Finding | Section | Data |
|---------|---------|------|
| Single-file pass rate | §127 | 5.8% (964 tasks) |
| Multi-file pass rate | §127 | 61.0% (254 tasks) |
| Sectioned synthesis introduction | §113 | 4.3% → 12.5% pass rate |
| File criteria introduction | §113 | 6.2% → 20.8% pass rate |
| Monolithic output ceiling | §109 | 47KB max per file |
| Sectioned output range | §109 | 61-143KB per file |
| C29 regression cause | §95 | Lost sectioned routing → 0% |
| All progress came from synthesis | §115 | Criteria rate flat at 84-86% since C11 |

### The Proposed Fix

For every single-file task:
1. Automatically create a companion "analysis-memo.docx" deliverable
2. Route through `synthesize_file_deliverables()` (the multi-file path)
3. Let the model split content between the primary deliverable and the analysis memo
4. The primary deliverable gets full file-scoped synthesis with artifact contracts

This requires NO changes to the scoring pipeline — the scorer evaluates each file independently. Adding a companion memo doesn't affect primary deliverable scoring.

### Alternative: Lower SECTION_THRESHOLD to 15

If adding companion files is too invasive, lowering SECTION_THRESHOLD from 40 to 15 would:
- Ensure more files exceed the threshold → more sectioned synthesis
- Increase output from 47KB (monolithic) to 60-140KB (sectioned)
- Preserve the existing pipeline architecture

The risk: over-sectioning could fragment coherent documents. But the empirical evidence (§109) shows sectioned outputs are both smaller AND more complete than monolithic outputs.

---

## 129. C8→C10 "Regression" Was a Task Set Change, Not a Real Regression

The apparent regression from C8 (6.4%, 3/47 pass) to C10 (0%, 0/48 pass) is misleading. The 3 tasks that passed in C8 (corporate-ma/review-enterprise-saas, energy/analyze-counterparty-markup-concession, IP/draft-claim-construction-brief) are NOT PRESENT in the C10 task set.

The smoke_48_* runs used a different 48-task subset than cycle10+. When the task set was standardized at cycle10, the 3 easy tasks that happened to pass were replaced by different tasks.

This means: **there was no regression**. C10 simply started with a harder task set. The "0% → 0% → 2.2% → 12.5%" trajectory (C10 through C14) is the real baseline.

---

## 130. Cross-Benchmark Performance Summary (Updated)

| Benchmark | Tasks | Pass Rate | Avg Score | Domain |
|-----------|-------|-----------|-----------|--------|
| AMA-Bench (full) | 158 | 82.6% | 0.826 | Information recall/memory |
| AMA-Bench (smoke) | 3 | 100% | 1.0 | " |
| FinanceBench (smoke) | 2 | 100% | 1.0 | Financial numerics |
| HotpotQA (smoke) | 2 | 100% | 1.0 | Multi-hop reasoning |
| OfficeQA Pro (full) | 100 | **24%** | 0.24 | Cross-document numerics |
| OfficeQA Pro (smoke) | 3 | 33% | 0.33 | " |
| Harvey LAB (full) | 1,251 | **17%** | — | Legal document analysis |
| Harvey LAB (48-task) | 48 | **27.1%** | — | " (best subset cycle) |
| GAIA (smoke) | 3 | **0%** | 0.0 | Multi-step tool use |
| GAIA (ws2) | 3 | **0%** | 0.0 | " |

### Cross-Benchmark Insights

1. **The system excels at information recall** (AMA 82.6%) — extracting and remembering facts from documents is its core strength.

2. **The system fails at multi-step reasoning** (GAIA 0%) — tasks requiring tool use, web search, and chained reasoning are completely unsupported.

3. **Numerical precision is a systematic weakness** (OfficeQA 24%) — the triple lossy compression chain (§41) destroys exact numbers. Smoke test showed 2/3 numeric mismatches.

4. **Harvey LAB represents a middle ground** (17-27%) — requires both information recall (AMA-like) AND precision (OfficeQA-like) AND compositional writing. The system handles recall but fails on precision and composition.

5. **Small smoke tests overestimate performance** — AMA smoke (100%) vs full (82.6%). FinanceBench smoke (100%) likely wouldn't hold at scale. The GAIA smoke (0%) is accurately pessimistic.

---

## 131. Putting It All Together: The Unified Failure Model

After 130 sections of analysis across 80+ runs, 1,251 tasks, 15+ source modules, and 5 benchmarks, the system's failure modes reduce to a clear hierarchy:

### Layer 1: Architecture (accounts for 77% of failures)

**The single-file synthesis bottleneck** (§127): 964/1,251 tasks produce one file and pass at 5.8%. 254 tasks produce two files and pass at 61.0%. The dual codepath in synthesis.py is the root cause. Everything downstream (model choice, prompt engineering, feature flags) is noise compared to this structural divide.

### Layer 2: Synthesis Quality (accounts for remaining 15-20%)

**Within the multi-file path**, the 39% failure rate comes from:
- Stochastic item assignment (SECTION_THRESHOLD cliff, §86)
- Criteria rate plateau at 84-86% (§115) combined with binary scoring cliff (§86)
- 31 pure regressions per cycle from curation → assignment → synthesis randomness (§108)

### Layer 3: Extraction (accounts for 5-8%)

**Specific verb failures** (§123): identify (0.9%), compare (1.3%), extract (5.3%) tasks fail because worker prompts lack verb-specific enumeration/comparison behavior. The extraction itself achieves 84-86% criteria but can't reach the 95%+ needed for binary all-pass.

### Layer 4: Dead Code (accounts for lost opportunity)

**3,179 lines behind feature flags** (§104): Blackboard maintenance, debt sensors, derived work, source claim verification, survival traces, web search — all disabled. Some would help (maintenance, debt sensors), others are irrelevant (web search for closed-book tasks). The system has never been tested at full capability.

### The Fix Priority Stack

| Priority | Fix | Estimated Impact | Effort |
|----------|-----|-----------------|--------|
| 1 | Route all tasks through file-scoped synthesis | 5.8% → 30%+ single-file pass rate | Medium |
| 2 | Lower SECTION_THRESHOLD to 15 | Prevents stochastic routing flips | Trivial |
| 3 | Add verb-specific worker prompts | 0.9-5.3% → 10-20% for identify/extract/compare | Medium |
| 4 | Enable blackboard maintenance | Reduce observation dominance | Low |
| 5 | Fix state conversion parse errors | 62% parse error → <10% | Medium |
| 6 | Enable survival trace feedback | Post-hoc audit → real-time correction | High |
| 7 | Increase synthesis max_tokens | 16K → 32K monolithic cap | Trivial |
| 8 | Enable debt sensor execution | Detect → fix cross-document gaps | Medium |

Fixes 1+2 alone could take the system from 17% to 35-40% at full benchmark scale. All 8 together could approach 60%.

---

## 132. Convergence Checker: Three-Gate Architecture and Its Failures

The convergence checker (`convergence.py`) implements a three-gate system that runs when the orchestrator proposes convergence (action: "converge") and the iteration count ≥ min_iterations (default 2):

### Gate 1: Structural Coverage (Deterministic)

Checks if extracted entries (observation, analysis, calculation) cover ≥50% of the document's `numbered_items` count from the structural profile. If entries < 50% of expected items, convergence is immediately rejected without spending any tokens.

**Problem**: The 50% threshold is extremely lenient. A task with 100 numbered clauses can converge with only 50 observations. But Harvey LAB scoring is binary all-pass — missing even ONE criterion fails the task. A 50% coverage gate in a system that needs ~100% coverage is structurally misaligned.

### Gate 2: Critical Signals (Deterministic)

If ANY signal has `status == "open"` AND `priority == "critical"`, convergence is blocked. This is the only hard block.

**Problem**: Only critical signals block. High/medium/low open signals are ignored. At convergence time, tasks typically have 28-96 open signals (see §137), many of which represent unfinished work. These are treated as acceptable losses.

### Gate 3: Adversarial Model Check (LLM-Based, 2048 max_tokens)

An LLM receives the full task instruction, document profiles, ALL active entries (up to 200K chars), entry type distributions, and open signal counts. It's instructed to "find reasons analysis is NOT complete." If it rejects, each `missing_work` item becomes a new Signal with type "convergence_gap" and priority "high".

**Problem**: In practice this gate almost never fires. Across 8 C27 tasks examined, ZERO convergence_gap signals were found in any final blackboard. The orchestrator appears to rarely propose convergence before hitting the max_iter cap.

### The Net Effect: Convergence Is Unused

All 8 tasks examined ran the full 12 iterations with only 35-57% budget used. The convergence checker's gates are architecturally correct but operationally irrelevant — the system hits the iteration ceiling long before it would trigger convergence.

| Task | Final Iter | Budget Used | Open Signals |
|------|-----------|-------------|--------------|
| draft-antitrust-compliance-training | 12 | 43.0% | 96 |
| extract-product-market-definitions | 12 | 42.9% | 40 |
| draft-emergency-application | 12 | 36.8% | 28 |
| scenario-01 | 12 | 44.6% | 73 |
| draft-commitment-letter | 12 | 48.3% | 32 |
| draft-intercreditor-agreement | 12 | 34.7% | 39 |
| draft-plan-of-reorganization | 12 | 56.6% | 35 |
| identify-counterparty-issues | 12 | 53.4% | 62 |

**Mean budget at exhaustion: 45.0%. Mean open signals: 50.6.** The system has more than half its budget remaining when forced to stop, and leaves 50+ pieces of identified work unaddressed.

---

## 133. analytical_steering() — Dead Code That Targets the Top Failure Modes

`convergence.py` lines 176-246 define `analytical_steering()`, a function designed to generate high-value analytical tasks every 4th iteration. It produces 3-5 worker tasks of types:

- **CALCULATIONS** — totals, percentages, ratios, deltas, HHI, financial exposure
- **CROSS-DOCUMENT COMPARISONS** — conflicts, inconsistencies across documents
- **ISSUES** — legal risks, missing provisions, problematic clauses
- **RECOMMENDATIONS** — remediation, suggested changes

This function is **exported from the module but NEVER CALLED** in the main loop. `grep` confirms zero call sites outside its own definition.

### Why This Matters

The top failure verb categories (§123) are:
- `identify` (0.9% pass rate) — needs exactly the ISSUES work type
- `compare` (1.3% pass rate) — needs exactly the CROSS-DOCUMENT COMPARISONS type
- `extract` (5.3% pass rate) — needs exactly the CALCULATIONS type

The dead code TARGETS the system's worst failure modes. It was designed to inject cross-document reasoning, calculation tasks, and issue identification — precisely the analytical work that workers don't generate spontaneously.

### Impact Estimate

If analytical_steering ran every 4th iteration (iterations 4, 8, 12), it would inject 9-15 targeted analytical tasks into the loop. At the current 84-86% criteria rate, even partial completion of these tasks could improve identify/compare/extract verb categories significantly.

---

## 134. Signal Prioritizer: The 20-Signal Batch Limit

`signals.py` implements `prioritize_signals()`, which assigns priority levels (critical/high/medium/low) to signals via an LLM call with 2048 max_tokens.

### The 20-Signal Cap

Only the first 20 signals per batch are processed: `signals[:20]`. In practice, signals are prioritized twice:
1. After initial reading — all open signals (typically 15-20, within the cap)
2. Each iteration — only newly created signals (typically 10-30)

The per-iteration batch is usually within the 20-signal cap, so this limit rarely truncates. But in iteration 7+ when signal generation accelerates, later-created signals may be skipped.

### Priority Distribution at Final Iteration (C27, 8 Tasks)

| Task | Critical | High | Medium | Low | Total |
|------|----------|------|--------|-----|-------|
| antitrust-training | 60 | 79 | 121 | 38 | 298 |
| product-market-definitions | 38 | 61 | 62 | 37 | 198 |
| emergency-application | 55 | 52 | 57 | 12 | 176 |
| scenario-01 | 60 | 88 | 62 | 2 | 212 |
| commitment-letter | 52 | 72 | 62 | 20 | 206 |
| intercreditor-agreement | 50 | 60 | 62 | 8 | 180 |
| plan-of-reorganization | 47 | 74 | 37 | 2 | 160 |
| counterparty-issues | 76 | 89 | 95 | 13 | 273 |

**Key finding**: Critical + high signals outnumber medium + low in most tasks. The system generates urgent work faster than it can complete it. Approximately 30% of all signals are critical, 37% high, 27% medium, 6% low.

---

## 135. Signal Lifecycle: Creation, Expiry, and the Information Loss Chain

Tracking signal state across all 12 iterations for one representative task (draft-antitrust-compliance-training-materials):

| Iter | Entries | Signals | Open | Expired | Addressed |
|------|---------|---------|------|---------|-----------|
| 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 1 | 238 | 17 | 15 | 0 | 2 |
| 2 | 261 | 47 | 41 | 0 | 6 |
| 3 | 282 | 71 | 52 | 0 | 19 |
| 4 | 315 | 87 | 45 | 11 | 31 |
| 5 | 333 | 106 | 40 | 27 | 39 |
| 6 | 351 | 128 | 41 | 40 | 47 |
| 7 | 376 | 164 | 62 | 47 | 55 |
| 8 | 397 | 190 | 70 | 57 | 63 |
| 9 | 412 | 206 | 70 | 67 | 69 |
| 10 | 431 | 236 | 68 | 91 | 77 |
| 11 | 449 | 262 | 74 | 102 | 86 |
| 12 | 474 | 298 | 96 | 109 | 93 |

### Five Critical Observations

1. **Signal generation outpaces signal resolution**: New signals per iteration (~25) consistently exceed addressed signals per iteration (~8). The backlog grows monotonically.

2. **Open signals NEVER decrease**: Despite addressing ~93 signals and expiring ~109 over 12 iterations, 96 open signals remain at the end. The system creates more work than it can complete.

3. **Expiry starts at iteration 4**: Medium/low priority signals expire after 3 iterations (blackboard.py line 152). By iteration 12, 109 signals (37% of all signals) have expired — representing work that was identified but NEVER done.

4. **Entry growth is linear (~20/iter)**: No acceleration or deceleration. Each iteration adds roughly the same number of entries regardless of how many signals are open.

5. **The system ends at max_iter, not at equilibrium**: With 96 open signals and 57% budget remaining, the system is forcibly stopped mid-analysis. It's nowhere near convergence.

### The Information Loss Is Structural

Of 298 total signals generated:
- **93 addressed (31%)** — work completed
- **109 expired (37%)** — work identified but LOST to 3-iteration timeout
- **96 open (32%)** — work identified but ABANDONED at iteration cap

**Only 31% of identified work is actually completed.** 69% of all signals are either expired or abandoned. This represents a massive information loss — the system discovers what needs to be done but lacks the capacity to do it.

---

## 136. The Zero Cross-Reference Problem (Confirmed at 4,834 Entries)

Across 8 C27 tasks containing 4,834 total entries, the cross-referencing fields on Entry are:

| Field | Entries With Non-Empty | Percentage |
|-------|----------------------|-----------|
| supports_entries | 0 | 0.0% |
| contradicts_entries | 0 | 0.0% |
| supersedes_entries | 0 | 0.0% |

**Every single entry across all tasks has empty supports, contradicts, and supersedes lists.** The Entry model defines these fields (models.py lines 130-132), the blackboard has logic to handle contradictions (lines 190-214), and the convergence checker counts "disputed" entries — but workers NEVER populate these fields.

### Entry Status Is Uniformly Active

| Status | Count | Percentage |
|--------|-------|-----------|
| active | 4,834 | 100.0% |
| disputed | 0 | 0.0% |
| superseded | 0 | 0.0% |

Not a single entry has been disputed or superseded. The blackboard is append-only — entries are added but never challenged, consolidated, or replaced.

### Implications for Quality

1. **No contradiction detection**: Even when documents contain conflicting information, the system never flags it. Workers don't use `contradicts_entries`.

2. **No evidence chain**: The `supports_entries` field would allow building dependency chains between observations and analyses. Without it, every entry is an isolated atom with no provenance beyond its source document.

3. **No supersession**: When a later analysis refines or corrects an earlier one, the old entry stays active at full confidence. Both the old (wrong or incomplete) and new (correct) entries flow into synthesis with equal weight.

4. **Blackboard maintenance would help**: The disabled `blackboard_maintenance` feature flag (§104) includes consolidation and cleanup logic. With zero cross-references, there's nothing to consolidate — but enabling it wouldn't help unless workers are first taught to populate cross-reference fields.

### Root Cause

Workers are given prompts that ask them to produce entries with these fields, but the model consistently ignores them. The worker prompt structure doesn't emphasize or demonstrate cross-referencing. The model defaults to producing isolated observations because that's the path of least resistance.

---

## 137. Entry Type Distribution: Observation Dominance Across All Tasks

At the final iteration (iter 12) across 8 C27 tasks:

| Task | Total | Obs % | Ana % | Calc % | Strat % | Gap % |
|------|-------|-------|-------|--------|---------|-------|
| antitrust-training | 497 | 67.6% | 4.8% | 5.4% | 0.8% | 9.1% |
| product-market-defs | 770 | 57.5% | 11.2% | 20.1% | 0.0% | 3.1% |
| emergency-application | 638 | 67.9% | 11.8% | 9.2% | 0.6% | 3.4% |
| scenario-01 | 482 | 56.4% | 12.2% | 6.0% | 1.5% | 7.7% |
| commitment-letter | 522 | 56.9% | 9.2% | 4.6% | 3.1% | 10.9% |
| intercreditor-agreement | 342 | 48.5% | 11.4% | 1.5% | 4.4% | 8.8% |
| plan-of-reorganization | 1080 | 72.9% | 1.6% | 16.6% | 0.0% | 3.2% |
| counterparty-issues | 1193 | 70.2% | 6.0% | 16.2% | 1.3% | 2.8% |
| **Mean** | **690** | **62.2%** | **8.5%** | **10.0%** | **1.5%** | **6.1%** |

### The Observation-to-Analysis Ratio Problem

On average, observations outnumber analyses **7.3:1**. The system extracts facts from documents far more than it reasons about them. This connects directly to the verb failure patterns (§123):

- **draft** (34.8% pass) — requires synthesis OF analysis, gets synthesis of raw observations
- **analyze** (29.9% pass) — explicitly needs analysis entries, gets mostly observations
- **identify** (0.9% pass) — needs issue identification (analysis/strategy), gets observations
- **compare** (1.3% pass) — needs cross-document analysis, gets per-document observations

### Gap Entries Signal Awareness Without Action

Gap entries average 6.1% of all entries — the system KNOWS it has incomplete coverage but doesn't act on it differently from any other entry type. Gaps flow into curation and synthesis alongside observations, with no special handling to prioritize filling them.

### Calculation Entries Vary Wildly

Tasks with numerical content (product-market-definitions at 20.1%, plan-of-reorganization at 16.6%, counterparty-issues at 16.2%) generate more calculation entries. But tasks requiring calculations that don't have explicit numbers in documents (antitrust-training at 5.4%, commitment-letter at 4.6%) produce very few.

The disabled `analytical_steering()` function (§133) would address this by injecting targeted calculation and analysis tasks.

---

## 138. Section Indexer: Regex Chunking and the Full Document Fallback

`section_index.py` builds the section index used by initial reading, worker dispatch, and synthesis. It uses 8 regex patterns to detect section boundaries:

| Level | Pattern | Matches |
|-------|---------|---------|
| 1 | `^#{1}\s+(.+)$` | Single markdown heading |
| 1 | `^(ARTICLE\|Article)\s+[IVXLCDM\d]+` | Legal article markers |
| 2 | `^(SECTION\|Section)\s+[\d.]+` | Section markers |
| 2 | `^#{2}\s+(.+)$` | Double markdown heading |
| 3 | `^#{3}\s+(.+)$` | Triple markdown heading |
| 3 | `^\s*\d+\.\d+\s+[A-Z]` | Numbered subsections |
| 1 | `^(EXHIBIT\|Exhibit\|SCHEDULE\|Schedule)\s+[A-Z\d]` | Exhibits/schedules |
| 1 | `^#\s*Page\s+\d+` | Page breaks |

### Hardcoded Limits

- **Section name truncation: 120 characters** — long legal headings get cut
- **Section text resolution: 24,000 characters default** — matches initial reading chunk size
- **Full Document fallback**: If no section patterns match, the ENTIRE document becomes one section at level 1, named "Full Document"

### The PDF Conversion Problem

All Harvey LAB source documents are PDFs converted to text. The conversion quality determines whether regex patterns match:

1. **Well-structured contracts** with "Article I", "Section 1.1" formatting → good section detection
2. **Scanned/OCR documents** → heading structure may be lost → Full Document fallback
3. **Multi-column layouts** → text ordering may be scrambled → incorrect section boundaries

When the Full Document fallback triggers, initial reading receives the entire document as one 24K-char chunk (with 2K overlap between chunks). This means all initial observations come from one undifferentiated blob rather than targeted section reads.

### Impact on Extraction Quality

The section index determines:
1. What sections workers are dispatched to read
2. How the orchestrator references specific document regions
3. What evidence provenance is recorded in entries

Poor section detection → poor provenance → workers can't target specific sections → observations lack structural context → synthesis can't organize by section.

---

## 139. Supervisor Review: The Approving Rubber Stamp

The supervisor review (`convergence.py` lines 88-173) runs post-loop as a quality gate. Two rounds maximum, each allowing up to 3 extra iterations if rejected.

### The Approval Bias

The supervisor prompt contains explicit approval bias:
- "YOUR DEFAULT SHOULD BE APPROVE"
- Rejection requires gaps that are simultaneously MATERIAL, SPECIFIC, and ACTIONABLE
- The triple requirement (all three conditions) makes rejection structurally difficult

### Empirical Evidence

All 8 C27 tasks have a `supervisor_approved_0.json` file, indicating supervisor APPROVED on the FIRST round. Zero tasks were rejected by the supervisor.

This means the supervisor review is functionally a rubber stamp. It never triggers the 3-extra-iteration re-work loop. The system goes straight from the 12-iteration cap to post-processing.

### Contrast with Signal State

At the time of supervisor approval:
- 28-96 open signals remain (identified but unaddressed work)
- 109+ signals have expired (work lost to the 3-iteration timeout)
- Budget usage is 35-57% (more than enough capacity for additional work)

The supervisor sees all this and still approves. The "default APPROVE" prompt instruction overrides what would otherwise be clear evidence of incomplete analysis.

### The Budget-Gate Interaction

Even if the supervisor rejected, the budget gate at 90% (`if budget_used_pct() >= 90`) would cap additional work. But since tasks only use 35-57% of budget, there's 33-55% budget headroom that the supervisor could utilize. The approval bias means this headroom is never used.

---

## 140. XLSX Output Tasks: The 41.7% Pass Rate Anomaly (C23, 1,251 Tasks)

Of the 1,251 tasks in C23, 24 produce at least one .xlsx output file. Their pass rate is dramatically higher:

| Output Type | Tasks | Pass | Rate | Criteria Rate |
|------------|-------|------|------|--------------|
| Has XLSX | 24 | 10 | **41.7%** | ~85% |
| No XLSX | 1,108 | 195 | **17.6%** | ~83% |
| **Ratio** | | | **2.4x** | |

### Single-File XLSX Tasks Pass at 75%

Breaking down by file count:

| File Count | Tasks | Pass | Rate |
|-----------|-------|------|------|
| 1 file (xlsx only) | 4 | 3 | **75.0%** |
| 2+ files (xlsx + docx) | 20 | 7 | **35.0%** |

Three of four single-file xlsx tasks pass — a rate **12.9x higher** than the overall single-file rate of 5.8%. This suggests xlsx output is inherently more compatible with the synthesis pipeline than docx.

### Why XLSX Tasks Outperform

1. **Structured output format**: Spreadsheets are rows and columns, not prose. The synthesis pipeline's weaknesses (truncation, section stitching, coherence) are less damaging when output is tabular.

2. **Extraction-aligned tasks**: Most xlsx tasks are extraction-focused (extract-ballot-tallies, extract-penalty-calculations, extract-commitments), which aligns with the system's strongest capability — information recall (§130, AMA 82.6%).

3. **Lower criteria complexity**: Xlsx task criteria tend to check for presence of specific data points, not prose quality or document structure.

### The 14 Failing XLSX Tasks: Near-Misses

| Criteria Rate | Count | Range |
|--------------|-------|-------|
| 95-99.4% | 7 | 1-5 criteria short |
| 85-95% | 3 | 5-15 criteria short |
| Below 85% | 4 | 15+ criteria short |

7 of 14 failing xlsx tasks are within 5 criteria of passing. These near-misses represent the easiest improvement targets across the entire benchmark.

### Outlier: draft-disclosure-schedule-preparation

This task produces **43 output files** (8 xlsx + 35 docx) — the most complex task in the benchmark. It fails at 167/168 criteria (99.4%), missing just ONE criterion. This is likely a stochastic loss — on a second run, it might pass.

## 141. Per-Category × Per-Verb Matrix: The Complete Performance Map (C23, 1,251 Tasks)

The definitive verb × category performance matrix, covering all 24 categories and 7 verb groups:

### Global Verb Performance

| Verb | Tasks | Pass | Rate | Criteria % |
|------|-------|------|------|-----------|
| draft | 474 | 165 | **34.8%** | 90.4% |
| analyze | 97 | 29 | **29.9%** | 85.4% |
| review | 82 | 9 | **11.0%** | 81.2% |
| other | 85 | 7 | **8.2%** | 84.1% |
| extract | 150 | 8 | **5.3%** | 75.1% |
| compare | 152 | 2 | **1.3%** | 76.7% |
| identify | 211 | 2 | **0.9%** | 74.4% |

Draft tasks dominate both volume (37.9% of benchmark) and performance (34.8% pass, 90.4% criteria). The verb hierarchy is nearly perfectly ordered by pass rate, with a clear tier structure:

- **Tier 1**: draft (34.8%), analyze (29.9%) — generative tasks the system handles
- **Tier 2**: review (11.0%), other (8.2%) — evaluation tasks that partially work
- **Tier 3**: extract (5.3%), compare (1.3%), identify (0.9%) — precision tasks that nearly always fail

### Top-Performing Category × Verb Cells (≥3 tasks, ≥50% pass)

| Category | Verb | Pass/Total | Criteria % |
|----------|------|-----------|-----------|
| trusts-estates / analyze | 3/3 | 100% |
| environmental-esg / analyze | 3/3 | 100% |
| structured-finance / analyze | 2/2 | 100% |
| emerging-companies / draft | 17/26 | 96.7% |
| data-privacy / draft | 9/11 | 98.5% |
| banking-finance / draft | 6/11 | 96.2% |
| trusts-estates / draft | 19/32 | 95.0% |
| funds-asset-mgmt / draft | 21/44 | 88.9% |

### Worst-Performing Category × Verb Cells (≥3 tasks)

| Category | Verb | Pass/Total | Criteria % |
|----------|------|-----------|-----------|
| funds-asset-mgmt / compare | 0/2 | 40.7% |
| capital-markets / compare | 0/4 | 48.4% |
| banking-finance / analyze | 0/2 | 58.1% |
| structured-finance / identify | 0/6 | 58.3% |
| capital-markets / review | 0/6 | 61.8% |

### Category-Level Insights

- **emerging-companies-venture-capital** is the easiest category: 18/43 pass (41.9%), driven by draft tasks at 96.7%
- **white-collar-defense** is the hardest: 0/21 pass (0%), despite reasonable criteria rates (~75-92%)
- **corporate-ma** has the most tasks (161) and passes 21 (13.0%), dragged down by identify (0/19) and extract (0/6)

### The "Other" Verbs (85 Tasks, 7 Pass)

The "other" bucket contains: assess (22), research (10), summarize (8), triage (8), build (5), map (5), prepare (4), respond (2), synthesize (2), audit (2), plus 12 singletons. Only 7/85 pass (8.2%).

## 142. Cycle Variant Sensitivity: Same Code, Different Results

Comparing cycle variants reveals high configuration sensitivity even on identical code and task sets.

### cycle23: general_artifact_contracts vs full_all (Same Hash e6d1a70, Same 48 Tasks)

| Config | Pass Rate |
|--------|-----------|
| general_artifact_contracts | **11/48 (22.9%)** |
| full_all (same 48 tasks) | **8/48 (16.7%)** |

5 tasks flipped between configs:

| Task | general_artifact | full_all | Delta |
|------|-----------------|----------|-------|
| immigration/draft-response-to-notice | PASS (60/60) | FAIL (33/60) | **-27 criteria** |
| real-estate/draft-commercial-lease | PASS (107/107) | FAIL (95/107) | **-12 criteria** |
| immigration/extract-eligibility | PASS (48/48) | FAIL (47/48) | -1 criterion |
| employment-labor/draft-updated-anti | PASS (60/60) | FAIL (59/60) | -1 criterion |
| corporate-ma/draft-board-deck | FAIL (131/132) | PASS (132/132) | +1 criterion |

The immigration/draft-response flip is extraordinary: **60/60 under one config, 33/60 under another**, on the same code and same task. This is a 45% criteria swing from config alone.

### cycle21b vs cycle22 (Different Hashes, Same 48 Tasks)

| Cycle | Pass Rate |
|-------|-----------|
| cycle21b (c1cd968) | **9/48 (18.8%)** |
| cycle22 (21f5f32) | **8/48 (16.7%)** |

3 tasks flipped:
- 2 passed in c21b but failed in c22 (by 1 criterion each)
- 1 passed in c22 but failed in c21b (36/63 → 63/63, a dramatic improvement)

### The Stable Core (6 Tasks Always Pass Across c21b/c22)

| Task | Always Passes |
|------|---------------|
| capital-markets/draft-indenture-for-senior-secured-notes | Yes |
| data-privacy/draft-markup-of-cross | Yes |
| ecvc/draft-voting-agreement | Yes |
| employment-labor/draft-updated-anti | Yes (except full_all c23) |
| funds-asset-mgmt/draft-lpa/scenario-10 | Yes |
| immigration/draft-response-to-notice | Yes (except full_all c23) |
| ip/draft-bilateral-nda | Yes |

All stable passes are **draft** verb tasks. No extract, compare, identify, or review task is a stable pass.

### Implications

1. **Config sensitivity is high**: The same code can produce 6pp swings (16.7% → 22.9%) on the same tasks.
2. **Stochastic variance is ±1 criterion**: Most flips are by exactly 1 criterion, confirming the binary scoring cliff (§86).
3. **Some tasks are catastrophically sensitive**: immigration/draft-response swings from 33/60 to 60/60 on the same code.
4. **The general_artifact_contracts config is strictly better**: +3 net passes over full_all on the same code and tasks.

7 runs containing 195 task results exist without scores. The most significant loss is the two cycle18 inferred_file_contracts variants — these represent the earliest experiments with file-scoped contracts, a key architectural feature. Without scores, we can't measure whether the first implementation helped or hurt.

The only scored cycle18 variant (cycle18b_inferred_contracts_no_criteria_ffd79c9) shows **0/48 pass (0.0%)**, suggesting the initial file contracts implementation was broken.

## 144. The 48-Task Smoke Set: Cross-Cycle Trajectory (Expanded)

Combining all scored 48-task runs into a single trajectory:

| Cycle | Config | Hash | Pass | Rate |
|-------|--------|------|------|------|
| C18b | no_criteria | ffd79c9 | 0 | 0.0% |
| C21b | seed_blueprint_supported | c1cd968 | 9 | 18.8% |
| C22 | seed_guided_initial_reading | 21f5f32 | 8 | 16.7% |
| C23 | general_artifact_contracts | e6d1a70 | 11 | **22.9%** |

The trajectory from 0% (C18b) to 27.1% (C27) spans 5 scored snapshots. Progress is nonlinear:
- C18b → C21b: +18.8pp (the biggest single jump, from file contracts to seed blueprints)
- C21b → C22: -2.1pp (slight regression despite code changes)
- C22 → C23 general: +6.2pp (artifact contracts config improvement)
- C23 general → C27: +4.2pp (plan coverage repair)

The C23 full_all config at 16.7% shows that **config choice alone** can swing the 48-task set by 6.2pp (vs C23 general at 22.9%). Code improvements from C23 to C27 added another 4.2pp.

## 145. Entry Confidence Distribution: The 0.5 Default Problem

The Entry model defines a `confidence` field with default 0.5, clamped to [0.1, 0.98] by the blackboard (blackboard.py lines 185, 194, 196, 218). But what values do entries actually have?

Across 8 C27 tasks with 4,834 entries at iteration 9:
- 100% of entries are status "active"
- 0% have any cross-references (supports, contradicts, supersedes)
- Confidence values are set by workers, but without cross-referencing or dispute resolution, confidence has no operational effect — it's never used to filter, weight, or prioritize entries during synthesis

The confidence field feeds into the "disputed" detection threshold (entries with `status == "active"` AND `confidence < 0.4` are treated as disputed in the orchestrator context, blackboard.py lines 138-141). But since no entries are ever disputed or low-confidence, this detection never triggers.

### The Epistemic Status Model: Unused Rich Structure

Each entry has an `EpistemicStatus` with:
- `classification`: "inference", "direct_observation", etc.
- `source_credibility`: "unknown", "high", "low", etc.
- `motivation`: reasoning for the classification
- `neutral_restatement`: bias-neutral rephrasing

In practice, workers set classification and sometimes motivation, but `source_credibility` defaults to "unknown" for virtually all entries. The system has rich epistemic infrastructure that's never utilized for quality differentiation.

---

## 146. Signal Deduplication: Trigram Similarity at 0.5

New signals are deduplicated against existing signals using trigram similarity (blackboard.py lines 18-31). If a new signal's content has ≥0.5 trigram overlap with any existing signal, it's considered a duplicate and not added.

### The Threshold Tradeoff

At 0.5 (50% trigram overlap):
- **Too loose**: Signals phrased differently about the same topic might both be added, leading to redundant work items
- **Too tight**: Related but distinct signals might be rejected, losing nuance

Given that tasks end with 160-298 total signals (§135), the dedup is either too loose (allowing too many) or the system genuinely generates that many distinct work items. The 37% expiry rate suggests many of these signals represent valid but unaddressable work.

### Signal Type Monotony

Across all 8 C27 tasks examined, virtually 100% of signals are type "question". Zero "convergence_gap" signals (from the adversarial convergence check) and zero other types.

The signal system was designed to support multiple types (question, contradiction_resolution, convergence_gap, etc.) but in practice only generates questions. This means the signal prioritizer is ranking a homogeneous pool — there's no type-based routing or handling.

---

## 147. Near-Miss Tasks: 237 Tasks Within 5 Criteria of Passing (C23, 1,251 Tasks)

The most actionable data in the entire analysis — tasks that ALMOST pass but fail by 1-5 criteria:

### Cumulative Impact of Near-Miss Conversion

| Tier | Tasks | Cumulative Passes | Rate |
|------|-------|------------------|------|
| Current passes | 222 | 222 | 17.7% |
| +1 criterion fixes | 51 | 273 | **21.8%** |
| +2 criteria fixes | 44 | 317 | **25.3%** |
| +3 criteria fixes | 42 | 359 | **28.7%** |
| +4-5 criteria fixes | 100 | 459 | **36.7%** |

**Converting all 237 near-misses would nearly double the pass count from 222 to 459.** These tasks already achieve 95-99%+ criteria rates and fail only on the last few granular details.

### Near-Misses by Verb

| Verb | Near-Miss | Total | Current Pass Rate | Potential Rate |
|------|-----------|-------|------------------|----------------|
| draft | 102 | 474 | 34.8% | **56.3%** |
| compare | 36 | 152 | 1.3% | **25.0%** |
| identify | 30 | 211 | 0.9% | **15.2%** |
| extract | 18 | 150 | 5.3% | **17.3%** |
| review | 17 | 82 | 11.0% | **31.7%** |
| analyze | 10 | 97 | 29.9% | **40.2%** |

Compare and identify tasks have near-zero pass rates but significant near-miss counts (36, 30). These tasks are close to the line — they just need 1-5 more criteria addressed.

### Near-Misses by Category (Highest Conversion Rates)

| Category | Near-Miss Rate | Near-Misses | Total Failing |
|----------|---------------|-------------|---------------|
| data-privacy-cybersecurity | **52.9%** | 18 | 34 |
| immigration | **40.0%** | 10 | 25 |
| antitrust-competition | **38.7%** | 12 | 31 |
| corporate-governance | **29.9%** | 26 | 87 |
| trusts-estates | **29.1%** | 16 | 55 |

Data-privacy tasks are the easiest to flip — over half of failing tasks are within 5 criteria.

### What Do Near-Misses Fail On?

| Failure Pattern | Occurrences | % of 718 |
|----------------|-------------|----------|
| Missing specific issues (ISSUE_NNN) | 233 | 32% |
| Missing sections/deliverables | 100 | 14% |
| Legal citation requirements | 89 | 12% |
| Wrong/missing calculations | 70 | 10% |
| Missing severity/risk ratings | 47 | 7% |
| Formatting/structural | 41 | 6% |
| Distractor (false positive) | 4 | <1% |

**Failing criteria are overwhelmingly task-specific** — no single criterion recurs across many tasks. This means improvement must come from deeper, more thorough document analysis, not from fixing one systematic pattern.

### The 51 One-Criterion-Short Tasks

These are the absolute highest-ROI targets. Each needs exactly ONE more piece of evidence to pass. They represent 51 potential passes requiring the smallest marginal improvement per task.

---

## 148. Artifact Commitments: The C30 Pre-Declaration System

C30 introduced `artifact_commitments.json` — a pre-synthesis declaration of what calculations, findings, and statements MUST appear in each output file.

### Scale

| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Total commitments across 48 tasks | 1,205 |
| Mean per task | 25.1 |
| Most common cap | 30 (most tasks hit this) |
| Minimum | 4 (IP/draft-markup-of-counterparty-dpa) |

### Commitment Structure

Each commitment contains:
- `entry_id`: Source blackboard entry
- `importance`: high or critical
- `section`: "Required Calculations" or "Required Findings"
- `summary`: What must appear
- `verification_terms`: String patterns to match in output
- `target_file`: Which output file should contain this
- `native_form`: calculation_statement or memo_statement

### The Problem: No Post-Verification

artifact_commitments.json records what SHOULD appear, but there's no corresponding verification file showing what DID appear. The scoring is done independently by the judge (scores.json), so commitments are a pre-synthesis contract with no enforcement loop.

### Impact (§120 Revisited)

C30 artifact commitments were net negative (-3.0pp, 25 regressed vs 16 improved). The commitment system declares obligations but doesn't verify or enforce them. It adds constraint without feedback.

---

## 149. Debt Sensors: 329 Actionable Gaps, Zero Resolved

C30 also introduced `debt_sensors.json` — gap detection for analysis coverage. Every sensor across all 48 tasks has status `actionable_gap`. Zero sensors are resolved.

### Sensor Types

| Type | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `source_object/unread_section` | Sections of documents not fully read |
| `source_object/thin_source_coverage` | Documents read but not deeply analyzed |
| `source_object/missing_entity` | Critical entities not found in blackboard |
| `relation/conflict` | Conflicting findings across documents |
| `relation/reconciliation` | Unresolved tensions between sources |
| `relation/provision_interplay` | Unexplored cross-provision interactions |

### Distribution

- Total sensors: 329 across 48 tasks (mean 6.9 per task)
- Range: 1-13 per task
- **100% status: actionable_gap**

### Why All Sensors Are Unresolved

Debt sensors run AFTER the main swarm loop (they appear in `blackboard_iter_12_post_debt_sensors.json`). By the time they detect gaps, the system has already consumed its iterations and is heading to synthesis. The gaps are identified but there's no mechanism to act on them.

This is the same pattern as signal expiry (§135) — the system discovers work it can't complete. Debt sensors are a diagnostic tool, not a feedback loop.

### derived_work_items.json: Never Generated

Despite being a feature-gated capability (§104), `derived_work_items.json` was NOT found in any cycle. This diagnostic file is defined in code but never produced in any scored run.

---

## 150. Plan Coverage Review: Empty in C27, Rich in C23

A critical discrepancy between cycle configurations:

| Cycle | Config | Tasks with Reviews | Reviews Empty |
|-------|--------|-------------------|---------------|
| C23 general_artifact | e6d1a70 | 44/48 (92%) | 4/48 (8%) |
| C27 plan_coverage | 30fa25a | 0/48 (0%) | 48/48 (100%) |

In C23, plan coverage review actively evaluates 8-11 seed questions and 6-9 criteria per task. In C27, every task's review is `{seed_coverage: [], criteria_coverage: []}`.

### What C23 Reviews Detect

In C23 tasks that have reviews:
- All 11 passing tasks have non-empty reviews (100% coverage, no gaps)
- All 33 failing tasks with reviews show seed_coverage of 8-11 items and criteria_coverage of 6-9 items
- Reviews correctly identify specific gaps in analysis coverage

### Why C27 Reviews Are Empty

The C27 config change likely disabled or changed the plan_coverage_review configuration. This means C27's plan_coverage_repair feature (the cycle's namesake) is repairing against an EMPTY review — there are no gaps to repair because the review found nothing.

This explains why C27 still achieves 27.1% — the repair infrastructure exists but the review input is empty, so repair runs vacuously. The improvement from C23 to C27 came from OTHER changes, not from plan coverage repair.

---

## 151. C30 Diagnostic File Evolution: Three New Subsystems

C30 introduced three entirely new diagnostic file types compared to C27:

| File | C30 | C27 | Purpose |
|------|-----|-----|---------|
| artifact_commitments.json | 48/48 | 0/48 | Pre-synthesis obligation declarations |
| debt_sensors.json | 48/48 | 0/48 | Post-loop gap detection |
| prompt_audit.json | 48/48 | 0/48 | Prompt provenance and forbidden content tracking |

C30 also added a new blackboard pipeline stage: `blackboard_iter_12_post_debt_sensors.json`.

### The Feature Activation Trajectory

| Cycle | Features Active | Pass Rate |
|-------|----------------|-----------|
| C18b | Baseline | 0.0% |
| C21b | Seed blueprints | 18.8% |
| C22 | Guided initial reading | 16.7% |
| C23 | Artifact contracts + plan coverage review | 22.9% |
| C27 | Plan coverage repair (but empty reviews) | 27.1% |
| C30 | Artifact commitments + debt sensors + prompt audit | 0.0% |

C30's three new subsystems produced a CATASTROPHIC REGRESSION from 27.1% to 0.0%. The added complexity (artifact commitments, debt sensors) introduces constraint without feedback, killing every single pass from C27.

### The Pattern

Every feature addition follows the same trajectory:
1. Feature is built (code exists)
2. Feature is activated in a cycle
3. Feature either helps slightly or hurts
4. Feature is not iterated on — the next cycle adds MORE features instead

The codebase grows by adding new subsystems rather than deepening existing ones. This is the entropy pattern §7 in CLAUDE.md warns against.

---

## 152. Prompt Audit: The Benchmark Integrity System

C30 introduced `prompt_audit.json` — a system that tracks prompt provenance and checks for forbidden content (benchmark criteria leaking into generation prompts).

This is an integrity safeguard: generation must NEVER see rubric criteria, match_criteria, scorer outputs, task IDs, or evaluator-only metadata. The prompt audit verifies this constraint.

Without examining the audit file contents in detail, this system addresses a real concern — if scoring criteria leaked into generation prompts, pass rates would be inflated without genuine improvement.

---

## 153. The Complete Diagnostic Pipeline Map (Updated with C30)

Combining §99 with C30 discoveries, the full pipeline now has 25+ stages:

```
1. Task ingestion → document loading → format detection
2. Section indexing (regex patterns, §138)
3. Structural profiling (numbered_items count)
4. Initial reading (24K chunks, 2K overlap, max 10 workers)
5. Signal creation → signal prioritization (20-signal batches, §134)
6. Signal expiry (medium/low after 3 iterations, §135)
7. Orchestrator decision (4096 max_tokens, context limits)
   ├── 7a. Gap detection (70% threshold, redundant with Phase 4b)
   └── 7b. Fallback workers (5 default if model unparseable)
8. Worker dispatch → entry creation
9. Cross-reference check (UNUSED — §136)
10. Convergence check (3-gate: structural + critical signals + adversarial)
    └── 10a. analytical_steering (DEAD CODE — §133)
11. Loop iteration (max 12, min 2, budget gate 85%)
12. Supervisor review (default APPROVE bias, §139)
    └── 12a. Re-work loop (up to 3 extra iterations, budget gate 90%)
--- POST-LOOP ---
13. State conversion (observation → analysis upgrade, 62% parse error)
14. Plan coverage review (EMPTY in C27, populated in C23)
15. Plan coverage repair (50% parse error, §125)
16. Debt sensors (C30+, all actionable_gap, §149)
17. Obligations extraction
18. Curation (must_include min 20 items, §106)
19. Artifact commitments (C30+, pre-synthesis declarations, §148)
20. Synthesis routing (file-scoped vs monolithic, SECTION_THRESHOLD=40)
21. Item assignment (per-file allocation)
22. Sectioned synthesis OR monolithic synthesis
23. Source claim verification (DISABLED — §104)
24. Survival trace (WRITE-ONLY — §104)
25. Prompt audit (C30+, benchmark integrity, §152)
26. Output generation (docx/xlsx/md)
```

Of these 26 stages, 4 are completely unused (§9, §10a, §23, §24), 2 are write-only (§24, §25), 2 have 50-62% parse error rates (§13, §15), and 1 produces empty output in the latest cycle (§14). That's 9/26 stages (35%) that are non-functional or degraded.

---

## 154. Cross-Cycle Task Stability: 80% Deterministic (5 Scored Cycles)

Tracking each task across all scored 48-task cycles reveals the system is predominantly deterministic:

### Two Task Cohorts

The 5 scored cycles use TWO different 48-task sets:
- **Cohort A** (C21b, C22, C23): Same 48 tasks across 3 cycles
- **Cohort B** (C27, C30): Same 48 tasks across 2 cycles
- **Overlap**: Only 3 tasks appear in all 5 cycles

Total unique tasks across both cohorts: 93.

### Stability Classification

| Category | Count | Percentage |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| Stable Pass | 6 | 6.5% |
| Stable Fail | 68 | 73.1% |
| Stochastic | 19 | 20.4% |

**79.6% of tasks are deterministic** — they either always pass or always fail. Only 20.4% are stochastic, and most of that stochasticity comes from C30's total wipeout (see §155).

### Excluding C30

If we exclude C30 (which is an outlier at 0/48), stability looks even stronger:

| Category | Count | Percentage |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| Stable Pass | 19 | 20.4% |
| Stable Fail | 68 | 73.1% |
| Stochastic | 6 | **6.5%** |

Only 6 tasks (6.5%) are truly stochastic — they flip between pass and fail due to 1-criterion variance. The system is ~93% deterministic when excluding the C30 catastrophe.

### The 6 True Stochastic Tasks (All Cohort A)

| Task | c21b | c22 | c23 | Pattern |
|------|------|-----|-----|---------|
| ecvc/draft-emerging-companies | PASS | FAIL(55/56) | PASS | 1 criterion flip |
| immigration/extract-eligibility | PASS | FAIL(47/48) | PASS | 1 criterion flip |
| real-estate/draft-loan-agreement | FAIL(83/84) | FAIL(83/84) | PASS | 1 criterion short |
| real-estate/draft-commercial-lease | FAIL(106/107) | FAIL(104/107) | PASS | 1-3 criteria |
| capital-markets/draft-indenture | PASS | PASS | FAIL(77/83) | 6 criteria regression |
| struct-finance/extract-conditions | FAIL(36/63) | PASS | PASS | 27-criterion swing |

All 6 are near the pass/fail boundary. 4 of 6 flip by exactly 1 criterion — the binary scoring cliff (§86) turns microscopic quality variance into binary pass/fail flips.

---

## 155. C30 as Catastrophic Outlier: 0/48 Passes, Universal Regression

C30 (artifact commitments + debt sensors + prompt audit) achieved **0/48 passes** — a total wipeout. Every task that passed in C27 failed in C30.

### The 13 C27 Passes That Died in C30

All 13 tasks that passed C27 with 100% criteria dropped dramatically in C30:

| Task | C27 Criteria | C30 Criteria | Drop |
|------|-------------|-------------|------|
| funds/draft-subscription-agreement | 60/60 (100%) | 31/60 (51.7%) | **-48.3pp** |
| antitrust/draft-compliance-training | 60/60 (100%) | 32/60 (53.3%) | **-46.7pp** |
| banking/draft-commitment-letter | 77/77 (100%) | 46/77 (59.7%) | **-40.3pp** |
| funds/draft-lpa/scenario-21 | 125/125 (100%) | 79/125 (63.2%) | **-36.8pp** |
| ip/analyze-counterparty-markup-jda | 62/62 (100%) | 42/62 (67.7%) | **-32.3pp** |
| data-privacy/draft-external-privacy | 58/58 (100%) | 44/58 (75.9%) | **-24.1pp** |
| cap-markets/draft-equity-incentive | 85/85 (100%) | 65/85 (76.5%) | **-23.5pp** |
| insurance/analyze-reinsurance | 52/52 (100%) | 42/52 (80.8%) | **-19.2pp** |
| immigration/draft-response-rfe | 59/59 (100%) | 48/59 (81.4%) | **-18.6pp** |
| ip/draft-markup-dpa | 72/72 (100%) | 59/72 (81.9%) | **-18.1pp** |
| environmental/analyze-indemnity | 73/73 (100%) | 60/73 (82.2%) | **-17.8pp** |
| ecvc/draft-escrow-agreement | 77/77 (100%) | 68/77 (88.3%) | **-11.7pp** |
| real-estate/analyze-cre-loan | 68/68 (100%) | 63/68 (92.6%) | **-7.4pp** |

**Mean criteria drop: -26.8pp.** These are not random fluctuations — they are systematic, uniform degradation affecting EVERY previously-passing task.

### What C30 Changed

C30 enabled three new subsystems:
1. **Artifact commitments**: Pre-synthesis obligation declarations (1,205 commitments across 48 tasks)
2. **Debt sensors**: Post-loop gap detection (329 sensors, all unresolved)
3. **Prompt audit**: Benchmark integrity verification

The most likely cause of the regression is artifact commitments. By constraining synthesis to match pre-declared obligations, the system loses the flexibility that allowed high-quality output. The commitments add noise (incorrect or overly specific obligations) that synthesis must satisfy, degrading overall quality.

### C30 Criteria Rate Distribution

| Average criteria rate | C27 | C30 | Change |
|----------------------|-----|-----|--------|
| All 48 tasks | 83.0% | 73.7% | **-9.3pp** |
| 13 previously-passing | 100.0% | 73.5% | **-26.5pp** |
| 35 always-failing | 75.0% | 73.8% | **-1.2pp** |

The regression is concentrated in previously-passing tasks (-26.5pp) while always-failing tasks barely moved (-1.2pp). C30 specifically damaged tasks that were working well.

---

## 156. The Stable Core: 6 Tasks That Always Pass

Six tasks pass across all scored cycles (C21b, C22, C23 — excluding C30):

| Task | Verb | Files | Category |
|------|------|-------|----------|
| data-privacy/draft-markup-of-cross | draft | 2 | data-privacy |
| ecvc/draft-voting-agreement | draft | 2 | ecvc |
| employment/draft-updated-anti | draft | 2 | employment |
| funds/draft-lpa/scenario-10 | draft | 2 | funds |
| immigration/draft-response-to-notice | draft | 2 | immigration |
| ip/draft-bilateral-nda | draft | 2 | IP |

### Common Properties

1. **All are "draft" verb tasks** — no extract, compare, identify, or review task is a stable pass
2. **All produce 2 output files** — routing through file-scoped synthesis (§127)
3. **All achieve 100% criteria rate in every cycle** — not just passing, but perfect
4. **All are relatively simple task structures** — single-document or clear dual-document inputs

These tasks represent the system's sweet spot: draft-verb + 2-file output + moderate complexity. The pipeline works when all structural conditions align.

---

## 157. The 5 Highest-ROI Stable Fails: Always Above 95% Criteria

Five tasks consistently fail but always achieve >95% criteria rate — missing just 1-3 criteria every cycle:

| Task | Cycles | Mean Criteria | Typical Miss |
|------|--------|--------------|-------------|
| corporate-ma/analyze-qoe-reconciliation | A | 97.5% | 2-4 criteria |
| struct-finance/draft-credit-agreement-markup | A | 97.1% | 2-3 criteria |
| data-privacy/analyze-gdpr-amendment-impact | A | 96.7% | 1-3 criteria |
| corporate-ma/draft-board-deck | A | 96.5% | 3-5 criteria |
| data-privacy/draft-standard-contractual-clauses | B | 95.2% | 3-4 criteria |

These tasks are the absolute lowest-hanging fruit. They consistently achieve near-perfect scores but trip on the last few criteria. Fixing just these 5 would add ~5pp to the 48-task set.

### The 3 All-Cycle Tasks: Stable but Not Improving

Three tasks appear in ALL 5 cycles and never pass:

| Task | C21b | C22 | C23 | C27 | C30 | Trend |
|------|------|-----|-----|-----|-----|-------|
| banking/draft-intercreditor | 82.9% | 77.1% | 74.3% | 85.7% | 80.0% | Flat ~80% |
| environmental/identify-issues-eis | 90.9% | 88.6% | 88.6% | 84.1% | 84.1% | Slight decline |
| trade-sanctions/draft-restricted-party | 91.1% | 92.2% | 95.6% | 91.1% | 94.4% | Flat ~93% |

The trade-sanctions task is particularly interesting: 93% average criteria rate across 5 cycles, consistently just 4-8 criteria short. It's been stable for months with zero improvement despite multiple pipeline changes.

---

## 158. The Two-Cohort Problem: Limited Cross-Cycle Comparison

A methodological limitation: the 5 scored cycles use two barely-overlapping task sets.

| Cohort | Cycles | Tasks | Overlap |
|--------|--------|-------|---------|
| A | C21b, C22, C23 | 48 | 48 shared across 3 cycles |
| B | C27, C30 | 48 | 48 shared across 2 cycles |
| A ∩ B | All 5 | 3 | Only 3 tasks in both cohorts |

This means:
1. **Cohort A stability** (3-way) is well-measured: 6 stable passes, 6 stochastic, 36 stable fails
2. **Cohort B stability** (2-way, one of which is 0/48) is poorly measured: C30's total wipeout makes it impossible to distinguish "genuinely unstable" from "C30 killed everything"
3. **Cross-cohort trends** can only be measured on 3 tasks — too small for statistical significance

To properly measure stability, the same 48 tasks need to be scored across many cycles. The cohort switch between C23 and C27 makes long-term task tracking impossible for most tasks.

---

## 159. Curation Module: Architecture, Quality Gates, and Failure Modes

`curation.py` (190 lines) is the bridge between the swarm's blackboard entries and synthesis. It decides what facts go into the final output.

### Architecture

```
Active entries → Cluster by document → Curate each cluster (parallel)
                                        ↓
                        Model selects must_include items per cluster
                                        ↓
                        Aggregate all clusters → Density gate (≥20 items?)
                                        ↓
                              Yes → proceed    No → Fallback re-curation
                                        ↓
                        Merge with obligations → Merge with artifact commitments
                                        ↓
                        Route to synthesis (sectioned if >40 items, monolithic if ≤40)
```

### Hardcoded Limits

| Constant | Value | Purpose |
|----------|-------|---------|
| MIN_MUST_INCLUDE | 20 | Minimum items to pass density gate |
| SECTION_THRESHOLD | 40 | Sectioned synthesis above this count |
| SECTION_CHUNK_SIZE | 25 | Items per section in sectioned synthesis |
| SECTION_DRAFT_MAX_TOKENS | 8192 | Max tokens per section draft |
| ASSIGNMENT_BATCH_SIZE | 50 | Items per file assignment batch |
| SECTION_EVIDENCE_CHARS | 24,000 | Evidence text limit per section |
| SELECTED_ITEM_SUMMARY_CHARS | 700 | Per-item summary char limit |
| SWARM_CURATION_WORKERS | 8 | Max parallel curation workers |
| Max tokens per model call | 16,384 | Main curation call |
| Entry summary truncation | 400 chars | Per-entry text in prompt |
| Prompt max entries | ~60,000 chars | Entry summaries in main; ~80,000 in fallback |

### The Six Exhaustive Enumeration Rules

The curation prompt explicitly instructs the model:
1. MUST include every finding with a specific fact
2. One fact = one must_include item (NO grouping)
3. must_include count ≥ distinct facts count
4. < 20 items is "almost certainly incomplete"
5. Each item = ONE verifiable fact (numbers, names, dates)
6. INCLUDE: dollar amounts, percentages, dates, deadlines, party names, defined terms, obligations, conditions, restrictions, representations, warranties

### The Fallback Trigger

If initial curation produces < 20 items AND there are ≥ 20 active entries:
- Fallback prompt: "curation step produced too few items. You must now enumerate EVERY fact"
- Target: at least `len(active) // 2` items
- Deduplicates by entry_id against initial items

### Deduplication Is Weak

- During curation: NO deduplication (items from different clusters may overlap)
- In fallback: Only by entry_id (different summaries of the same fact pass through)
- In pipeline: By first 60 chars of summary (obligations merge)
- No semantic deduplication exists — near-identical items with different phrasing both survive

### No Quality Filtering

All items from the model output are accepted as-is. There's no validation of:
- Whether the summary is factually grounded in the entry
- Whether the importance assignment is correct
- Whether the section grouping is coherent
- Whether items are actually distinct vs reformulations

Verification happens later in synthesis (`_verify_completeness`), but by then the curation decisions are baked in.

---

## 160. Prompt Audit: Benchmark Integrity Verification (C30)

C30's `prompt_audit.json` tracks every prompt's provenance and checks for benchmark contamination.

### Structure

Each audit record contains:
- `stage`: Which pipeline stage (e.g., "relation_debt_detection", "source_object_debt_detection")
- `prompt_sha256`: Hash for reproducibility
- `prompt_chars`: Prompt size (22K-30K chars typical)
- `provenance`: List of data sources used ("user.instruction", "swarm.blackboard", "source.documents", etc.)
- `forbidden_provenance_hits`: Any benchmark criteria that leaked into prompts (SHOULD BE EMPTY)
- `forbidden_text_hits`: Any forbidden text patterns found (SHOULD BE EMPTY)
- `redacted_excerpt`: Truncated prompt text for audit

### Findings Across C30 Tasks

All examined tasks show:
- **forbidden_provenance_hits: []** — no benchmark criteria leaking
- **forbidden_text_hits: []** — no forbidden text patterns found
- Provenance is clean: user.instruction + swarm.blackboard + source.documents + clean.professional_prior_dynamic

The prompt audit confirms benchmark integrity is maintained. Generation prompts never see rubric criteria, match_criteria, or evaluator metadata. This rules out benchmark contamination as a source of either pass or fail.

---

## 161. Curation-to-Synthesis Handoff: The SECTION_THRESHOLD Decision

The critical routing decision happens at synthesis.py line 53: `len(must_include) > SECTION_THRESHOLD (40)`.

### Empirical Observations (C27, 20 Tasks)

| Task | Clusters | Last Cluster Items | Sections | Pass |
|------|----------|-------------------|----------|------|
| draft-antitrust-training | 11 | 30 | 44 (sectioned) | PASS |
| extract-product-market | 17 | 43 | 118 (sectioned) | FAIL |
| draft-emergency-application | 13 | 36 | 90 (sectioned) | FAIL |
| scenario-01 | 8 | 24 | 65 (sectioned) | FAIL |
| draft-commitment-letter | 6 | 25 | 7 (sectioned) | PASS |
| draft-intercreditor | 7 | 22 | 43 (sectioned) | FAIL |
| draft-plan-of-reorg | 19 | 19 | 35 (sectioned) | FAIL |
| identify-counterparty-issues | 18 | 20 | 142 (sectioned) | FAIL |
| draft-equity-incentive | 8 | 33 | 13 (sectioned) | PASS |
| review-proxy-statement | 10 | 22 | 63 (sectioned) | FAIL |
| draft-action-by-incorporator | 16 | 100 | 129 (sectioned) | FAIL |
| draft-external-privacy | 9 | 20 | mono | PASS |
| draft-standard-clauses | 8 | 41 | 38 (sectioned) | FAIL |
| draft-escrow-agreement | 7 | 22 | 23 (sectioned) | PASS |
| research-non | 12 | 4 | 85 (sectioned) | FAIL |

### Key Observations

1. **Only 1 of 20 tasks uses monolithic synthesis** (draft-external-privacy). This task PASSES. Most tasks exceed the 40-item threshold and go sectioned.

2. **Section count varies wildly**: 7 to 142 sections for sectioned tasks. More sections = more content but also more fragmentation.

3. **Passing tasks tend to have fewer sections**: 7, 13, 23, 44 vs failing tasks with 35-142.

4. **The "latest_item_count" in curation_progress.json is per-cluster, not total**: So "items=30" means the LAST cluster produced 30 items, not 30 total.

5. **research-non has only 4 items from last cluster**: Likely triggered fallback curation but still failed.

---

## 162. The Obligation-Curation-Commitment Pipeline: Three Sources of must_include

The final must_include list fed to synthesis comes from three sources merged in sequence:

```
1. build_synthesis_obligations() → 20-60 obligation dicts
   └── From seed plan, task instruction analysis
   └── Importance: critical/high
   └── Dedup by summary[:60] against existing

2. curate_entries() → 20-100+ curated items
   └── From active blackboard entries clustered by document
   └── Importance: critical/high/medium
   └── No dedup during curation

3. build_artifact_commitments() → 5-20 commitment items (C30+)
   └── From pre-declared artifact obligations
   └── Has target_file assignment
   └── Inserted at position 0 (highest priority)
```

Total must_include can reach 45-180+ items. At SECTION_THRESHOLD=40, virtually all tasks go sectioned.

### The Triple Source Problem

Three independent systems generate items with overlapping scope:
- Obligations come from task analysis (what SHOULD be in the output)
- Curation comes from blackboard entries (what WAS extracted)
- Commitments come from pre-declared obligations (what MUST appear)

No system knows what the others generated. Deduplication is by summary[:60] for obligations and by entry_id for commitments, but semantic overlaps persist. The synthesis model receives a list that may contain 3 versions of the same fact from 3 sources.

---

Updating §104 with C30's feature state:

### Newly Enabled in C30

| Feature | Lines | Status in C30 |
|---------|-------|---------------|
| Artifact commitments | ~441-442 | **ENABLED** (1,205 commitments generated) |
| Debt sensors | ~390-401 | **ENABLED** (329 sensors, all unresolved) |
| Prompt audit | new module | **ENABLED** (48 audit records, all clean) |

### Still Disabled in C30

| Feature | Flag | Lines | Impact |
|---------|------|-------|--------|
| Blackboard maintenance | SWARM_ENABLE_BLACKBOARD_MAINTENANCE | ~368-371 | Would consolidate/clean entries |
| Derived work | SWARM_ENABLE_DERIVED_WORK | ~373-378 | Would generate follow-up tasks |
| Source claim verification | SWARM_ENABLE_SOURCE_CLAIM_VERIFICATION | source_claims.py | Would verify claims against evidence |
| Source claim quarantine | SWARM_SOURCE_CLAIM_QUARANTINE | source_claims.py | Would quarantine unsupported claims |
| Web search | SWARM_WEB_SEARCH | web_search.py | Irrelevant for closed-book tasks |
| analytical_steering() | Not flag-gated; just never called | convergence.py:176 | Would inject targeted analytical tasks |

### The Feature Activation Track Record

| Cycle | Features Enabled | Pass Rate Change |
|-------|-----------------|-----------------|
| C21b | Seed blueprints | +18.8pp |
| C22 | Guided initial reading | -2.1pp |
| C23 | Artifact contracts + plan coverage review | +6.2pp |
| C27 | Plan coverage repair (but reviews empty) | +4.2pp |
| C30 | Artifact commitments + debt sensors + prompt audit | **-27.1pp** |

The pattern is clear: features that CONSTRAIN output (artifact commitments) hurt. Features that ENRICH input (seed blueprints, guided reading) help. C30 went the wrong direction.
