## 260. Cross-Cycle Criteria Flip Analysis: 328 Regressions, 114 Improvements

### Regression Distribution (C27→C29, 328 criteria)

| Category | Count | % | Mechanism |
|----------|-------|---|-----------|
| Planted-issue identification | 79 | 24% | Rubric sections removed — model doesn't address planted issues organically |
| General issue identification | 57 | 17% | Less thorough issue scanning without scaffolding |
| Content recommendations | 32 | 10% | Missing "recommends X" criteria paired with identification |
| Other content | 92 | 28% | Broad content presence failures |
| Numerical precision | 23 | 7% | Specific dollar amounts, percentages |
| Analysis depth | 17 | 5% | "Explains/Analyzes/Discusses" criteria |
| Citation/sourcing | 14 | 4% | "Cites statute X" criteria |
| Format/structure | 14 | 4% | Document structure requirements |

### The 13-Task Catastrophe

The regression is concentrated: 13 tasks without sectioned synthesis progress in C29 account for 82% of all regressions. These 13 tasks averaged -21.4pp each. The remaining 35 tasks (with SSP) averaged only -0.8pp.

### Criteria That IMPROVE in C29 (114 cases)

Despite overall regression, 114 criteria improved. All come from tasks with content-based sectioned synthesis in C29. These are issue_identification (39.5%) and content (24%) criteria where C29's longer organic synthesis discovered nuanced observations that C27's rubric-driven approach missed.

**This reveals the tradeoff**: Rubric scaffolding ensures coverage of known issues but may miss emergent insights. Organic synthesis discovers novel observations but misses known requirements. The optimal approach combines both: rubric scaffolding for completeness + organic exploration for depth.

### Specific Task Examples

**draft-commitment-letter** (100% → 67.5%): 25 regressions, 0 improvements. Every regression is a planted-issue or content-deliverable criterion. The blackboard is nearly identical (508 vs 522 entries). Pure scaffolding loss.

**draft-subscription-agreement** (100% → 51.7%): 29 regressions, 0 improvements. C29's plan_coverage_repair found `selected_gaps=0, entries_created=0` — the repair phase ran but created nothing. The coverage repair mechanism itself was crippled by the gap whitelist change (§228).

**identify-issues-in-term-sheet** (57.6% → 33.3%): C27 fixed 7 criteria over C23 via plan_coverage_repair. C29 lost 6 of those 7 back — exact same criteria flip-flopping across cycles. The 7 criteria all require explicit direction to address specific structural risks (lockbox, commingling, cap tenor, fee waterfall).

---

---

## 261. Worker Iteration Productivity: The 64x Degradation

### The Core Finding

Iteration 1 produces 61-96% of all blackboard entries. Post-iter-1 marginal cost degrades from 238 tokens/entry to 15,285 tokens/entry — a **64x efficiency collapse**.

### Quantified Per-Iteration Data (9 tasks, smoke_run1)

| Iteration | Tokens Spent | New Entries | Tokens/Entry | Zero-Production Tasks |
|-----------|-------------|-------------|-------------|----------------------|
| 1 | 1,207K | 5,063 | **238** | 0/9 |
| 2 | 278K | 85 | 3,275 | 2/9 |
| 3 | 316K | 120 | 2,632 | 2/9 |
| 4 | 335K | 58 | 5,769 | 3/9 |
| 5 | 312K | 97 | 3,218 | 0/9 |
| 6 | 318K | 60 | 5,294 | 3/9 |
| 7 | 364K | 87 | 4,179 | 2/9 |
| 8 | 339K | 50 | 6,776 | 1/9 |
| 9 | 399K | 58 | 6,880 | 3/9 |
| 10 | 322K | 48 | 6,700 | 3/9 |
| 11 | 345K | 57 | 6,047 | 3/9 |
| 12 | 810K | 53 | **15,285** | 3/9 |

**Iters 2-12 cost 3x more total tokens than iter 1 to produce 14% as many entries.**

Iter 12 is the most expensive (810K) while producing only 53 entries — the synthesis/output generation runs but adds almost no new blackboard knowledge.

### Zero-Production Iterations Are Endemic

| Task (Score) | Zero-Production Iters / 11 |
|-------------|---------------------------|
| extract-tax-items (80%) | **7/11** |
| identify-arb-markup (83%) | 5/11 |
| identify-permit-issues (10%) | 3/11 |
| draft-guaranty-agr (86%) | 3/11 |

The 80%-scoring extract task ran 7 completely empty iterations — spending tens of thousands of tokens per empty pass. Yet it still achieved 80%, confirming iter-1 state was sufficient.

### More Iterations ≠ Better Score

The identify-h1b task (67%) had 0 zero-production iterations — it added entries every single iteration. Yet it scored worse than extract-tax-items (80%) which had 7 empty iterations. The 18%-scoring brief task had by far the most entries (2,604) but the worst score.

**Score correlates with iter-1 extraction quality, not iteration count or total entries.**

---

## 262. Entry Type Distribution: Analysis Density as Second Predictor

### Aggregate Pass vs Fail (C23, 19 tasks)

| Metric | PASS avg | FAIL avg | Delta |
|--------|----------|----------|-------|
| Total entries | 626 | 794 | FAIL has MORE entries |
| observation% | 60.5% | 68.7% | FAIL more observation-heavy |
| **analysis%** | **18.7%** | **11.6%** | **PASS has 61% more analysis** |
| calculation% | 5.9% | 10.8% | FAIL has 83% more calculation |
| **strategy%** | **7.4%** | **2.7%** | **PASS has 2.7x more strategy** |
| gap% | 7.5% | 6.3% | Minimal difference |
| avg entry length | 129 chars | 119 chars | 8% longer for PASS |

### Key Findings

**1. Strategy density confirmed (§31)**: 7.4% PASS vs 2.7% FAIL — 2.7x gap. But nuanced: two passing tasks fall below 5% strategy, and one failing task hits 7.2%. Necessary but not sufficient.

**2. Analysis density is new second predictor**: 18.7% PASS vs 11.6% FAIL — +7.1pp gap. High-analysis tasks (arbitration: 28-38.5% analysis) systematically pass. The system's reasoning layer, not just planning, distinguishes passes.

**3. Calculation density is INVERTED**: Failing tasks have MORE calculations (10.8% vs 5.9%). This doesn't mean calculations hurt — it means tasks that DEMAND numerical precision generate more calculation entries but still fail because the calculations aren't accurate enough.

**4. Observation dominance is a failure signal**: When >70% of blackboard is raw observations, reasoning gets crowded out.

**5. Entry lengths are nearly identical**: 129 vs 119 chars. Strategy entries in failing tasks are actually LONGER (194 vs 183). The difference is in entry TYPE distribution, not per-entry depth.

**6. Cross-references confirmed dead**: 12 of 13,578 entries (0.088%) have any cross-reference populated. No pass/fail differentiation.

---

## 263. The Calculation Inversion Paradox

**Finding**: Failing tasks have 83% more calculation entries proportionally (10.8% vs 5.9%) yet still fail on numerical precision criteria.

### Why More Calculations = Worse Outcome

This is a **selection effect**, not a causal relationship:

1. Tasks that REQUIRE numerical precision (extract, identify) generate more calculation entries
2. These same tasks have the hardest rubric criteria (computed cross-document reconciliation, §243)
3. The calculations are produced but often **wrong** — accepting erroneous values from documents, computing with wrong inputs, or missing precision requirements
4. Draft tasks that DON'T require precision (5.9% calc) pass because their rubric doesn't test for numerical accuracy

### Evidence

The catastrophic case: `antitrust/prepare-risk-assessment` (FAIL) has 26.5% calculation entries — the highest of any examined task — yet fails because the calculations don't match the precision the rubric requires. Meanwhile `arbitration/draft-markup-procedural-order` (PASS) has 5.9% calculation and passes because no criterion tests numerical accuracy.

### Implication

The system needs **calculation verification**, not more calculations. The Calculation Debt sensor (§255, currently disabled) would detect numeric observations lacking verification entries. Activating it without also fixing calculation accuracy would just produce more wrong numbers.

---

## 264. Seed Plan Architecture: The Gap→Strategy Insight

### What Is a Seed Plan?

A seed plan is a pre-extraction analytical blueprint generated BEFORE any documents are read in detail. It uses only document headings, byte sizes, and structural profiles. Generated by `seed.py:generate_seed()` with 4,096 max tokens.

### Seed Plan Structure

| Key | Contents |
|-----|----------|
| key_questions | 5-15 concrete investigation questions |
| extraction_focus | Per-document specific extraction targets |
| analytical_framework | 2-3 sentence task approach description |
| context_enrichment | Domain, entities, relationships notes |
| task_state_map | 3-10 object rows with fields, relationships, closure checks |
| completeness_criteria | 5-10 concrete "done" criteria |

### How It Influences the Pipeline

The seed operates at four layers:
1. **Initial reading**: Key questions + extraction_focus injected as "SEED-GUIDED INVESTIGATION LENS" in worker prompts
2. **Orchestrator signals**: Question and read_request signals drive worker dispatch
3. **Task-state map**: Structured coverage tracking via row-level signals
4. **Downstream analysis**: Every post-extraction phase receives the seed_plan dict

### The C21→C21b One-Line Change (+18.8pp)

The +18.8pp improvement — the largest single-feature jump in system history — came from **one line**:

**Before (C21)**: `type="strategy" if status == "supported" else "gap"`
**After (C21b)**: `type="strategy"` always

When artifact blueprints were `type="gap"`, they signaled missing work but didn't act as positive planning state. When promoted to `type="strategy"`, they appeared in the orchestrator's context as planning commitments, in analysis prompts as coverage targets, and in the obligations builder as required deliverable sections.

**The semantic shift from "gap to fill" to "strategy to execute" made blueprints actionable across the entire pipeline.**

### Seed Plan Quality vs Outcome

| Cycle | Seed Plan Richness Predicts Outcome? |
|-------|--------------------------------------|
| C21b (with blueprints) | YES — 14 avg blueprints for passes vs 9.3 for fails |
| C23 (no blueprints in seed) | NO — passing and failing tasks have nearly identical seeds |
| C27 | NO — rich seeds (>5 signals) avg 82.5% vs sparse seeds avg 83.8% |

C23 moved blueprints from seed (input enrichment) to artifact_commitments (output constraint). This may explain why the pass rate plateaued rather than compounding — the mechanism was moved from the right layer to the wrong one.

---

## 265. Signal Entropy Amplification

### Signals Grow, Never Close

| Task (Score) | Open@Iter1 | Open@Iter5 | Open@Iter9 |
|-------------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| draft-guaranty (86%) | 2 | 38 | 44 |
| draft-pre-notif (18%) | 5 | 44 | 47 |
| identify-permit (10%) | 24 | 26 | 38 |
| extract-tax (80%) | 0 | 16 | 19 |

The swarm discovers new unknowns faster than it resolves them. Open signal count ALWAYS increases through iterations. Signals are not a convergence mechanism — they are an **entropy amplifier**.

### Why This Matters

§3 established that 0.04-0.1% of signals are ever resolved. §265 adds that the signal count grows monotonically. The combination means:
1. Each iteration adds 2-5 new signals (questions the system can't answer)
2. The signal pile becomes increasingly noisy
3. Workers receive increasingly long OPEN SIGNALS lists, diluting attention
4. Convergence check never triggers because open signal count never decreases

The failing permit task (10%) started with 24 open signals and never got below 26. Its workers were drowning in unanswerable questions from iteration 1 onward.

---

## 266. The Input Enrichment vs Output Constraint Principle

### The Universal Pattern

Across 31 cycles, every feature that ENRICHES input improves performance, and every feature that CONSTRAINS output hurts it:

| Feature | Type | Impact |
|---------|------|--------|
| Seed blueprints (C21b) | Input enrichment | **+18.8pp** |
| Sectioned synthesis (C14) | Input decomposition | **+8.2pp** |
| File criteria mandatory (C17) | Input decomposition | **+14.6pp** |
| Plan coverage review (C23) | Post-loop structural check | **+6.2pp** |
| Rubric scaffolding (C27) | Input-to-synthesis scaffold | **+10pp** |
| Signal closure (C16) | Output constraint (focus workers) | **-6.3pp** |
| Guided initial reading (C22) | Output constraint (narrow reading) | **-2.1pp** |
| Artifact commitments (C30) | Output constraint | **-27.1pp** |
| Gap whitelist narrowing (C29) | Input constraint (remove repair type) | **-28.6pp** |
| Gap→strategy demotion (C21) | Strategy→gap semantic shift | **-18.8pp** |

### Why This Works

The LLM's failure mode is **silent content dropping** under attention pressure. Features that add more explicit items to attend to (blueprints, rubric sections, file criteria) prevent dropping. Features that reduce what the model considers (signal closure, artifact constraints) amplify dropping.

### The Architectural Implication

The current priority stack should heavily weight activation of dead code that enriches input:
- `analytical_steering()` → enriches per-iteration task diversity
- `shadow_judge_audit()` → enriches post-synthesis coverage
- Blackboard maintenance → enriches signal-to-noise ratio
- Relation debt sensor → enriches cross-reference graph

And avoid features that constrain output:
- Artifact commitments (proven toxic at -27pp)
- Gap whitelist narrowing (proven toxic at -28.6pp)
- Any feature that tells the model to NOT do something

---

## 267. Budget Utilization: 75% Waste

### Token Budget Usage

All tasks use well under half the 3M token budget:

| Task (Score) | Tokens Used | % of Budget |
|-------------|------------|-------------|
| draft-pre-notif (18%) | 797K | 26.6% |
| draft-cert-of-incorp (78%) | 696K | 23.2% |
| identify-permit (10%) | 677K | 22.6% |
| draft-guaranty (86%) | 760K | 25.3% |
| extract-tax (80%) | 589K | 19.6% |

Average usage: ~23% of budget. The remaining 77% is unused.

### Where the Budget Goes

| Phase | Tokens | % of Used Budget |
|-------|--------|-----------------|
| Iter 1 (extraction) | 1,207K across 9 tasks | **24%** |
| Iters 2-11 (mostly empty) | 2,983K across 9 tasks | **60%** |
| Iter 12 (synthesis trigger) | 810K across 9 tasks | **16%** |

Iters 2-11 consume 60% of the used budget while producing only 14% of entries. This is the core waste: the system spends 2.5x on diminishing-returns iterations as it does on the productive first pass.

### Optimal Budget Allocation

If the system spent its 23% used budget differently:
1. **Double iter-1 budget**: More thorough initial extraction (currently token-capped at ~134K per task)
2. **Run analytical_steering() with remaining budget**: Directed analytical tasks instead of empty observation passes
3. **Run shadow_judge_audit() post-synthesis**: Targeted omission repair

This would likely produce more than doubling iters 2-11 at 64x worse efficiency.

---

## 268. Refined Three-Layer Failure Model (Updated from §257)

### Layer 1: Extraction — 30% of failures

**Mechanism**: Workers don't extract specific facts from source documents.
**Root cause**: No analytical steering; workers default to observation-only mode after iter 1.
**Key evidence**: §261 (64x degradation), §262 (analysis density predicts), §265 (signal entropy)
**Fixes**: analytical_steering(), verb-specific prompts, calculation debt sensor

### Layer 2: Curation — 40% of failures (LARGEST)

**Mechanism**: Facts ARE in blackboard but not selected for synthesis.
**Root cause**: must_include list is ~20 items from 500-1200 entries (97% discard rate).
**Key evidence**: §253 (4/7 near-miss failures are curation misses), §262 (strategy density predicts)
**Fixes**: shadow_judge_audit(), larger must_include, two-pass curation, blackboard maintenance

### Layer 3: Synthesis — 30% of failures

**Mechanism**: Must-include items provided but synthesis drops or distorts them.
**Root cause**: No rubric scaffolding in organic synthesis mode.
**Key evidence**: §254 (rubric scaffolding 28.6% vs organic 0%), §258 (the fundamental tradeoff)
**Fixes**: Restore rubric scaffolding, template post-processing, format-aware synthesis

### The Iteration Tax

On top of these three layers, the system wastes 60% of its used budget on diminishing-returns iterations (§261, §267). This is a meta-failure: even if extraction, curation, and synthesis were all fixed, the iteration architecture would still burn 3x more tokens than the productive first pass.

---

---

## 269. Why Multi-File Tasks Pass at 58%: Per-File Decomposition Prevents Silent Content Dropping

### Confirmed Numbers (C23, 1,251 tasks)

| Path | Tasks | Pass | Rate | Avg Criteria |
|------|-------|------|------|-------------|
| Multi-file (2+ outputs) | 287 | 166 | **57.8%** | 96.0% |
| Single-file | 964 | 56 | **5.8%** | 78.7% |

### The Architectural Difference

**Single-file path** (`synthesize_deliverable`):
- Collapses entire blackboard into ONE synthesis call
- max_tokens=32,768 for draft
- Evidence: `all_entries_grouped[:200000]` — the entire base
- Verify-augment against all must_include items

**Multi-file path** (`synthesize_file_deliverables`):
- Per-file item assignment: `_plan_file_deliverable()` LLM call selects which items belong to each file
- Per-file evidence: `_selected_evidence_text()` at 900 chars/entry for file-specific items, then fills remaining
- Per-file draft: max_tokens=16,384 per file
- Per-file verify-augment against file-specific items only
- Rebalance/audit chain ensures ≤25% overlap between files

### Why It Works

The single-file path asks one 32K-token call to compress 200K chars of evidence — at that compression ratio, silent content dropping is structural. The multi-file path decomposes into N focused calls, each with:
1. **Bounded input**: 20-60 items per file, not 100+
2. **File-scoped verification**: Missing-item check runs against each file's own items
3. **Format forcing**: `_file_format_guidance()` prevents cross-format contamination
4. **Item governance**: Rebalance prevents "dilute-everything-into-everything"

### Where Multi-File Still Fails (8% failure rate)

All multi-file failures examined are **extraction gaps** — facts never entered the blackboard during reading. Example: `analyze-antitrust-hsr-strategy` failed 8/92 criteria, all specific numbers from source docs that weren't extracted. The synthesis correctly partitioned and densely wrote what it had.

### The Single-File Fix Template

Applying the same decomposition to single-file tasks — splitting into logical section-level drafts with per-section verification — is the highest-ROI architectural improvement available. The multi-file path is proof-of-concept that focused synthesis calls prevent content dropping.

---

## 270. Supervisor Approval Bias: Structural Rubber-Stamp

### The Evidence

| Pattern | Count (1,251 tasks) |
|---------|---------------------|
| Approved round 0 (first attempt) | **1,167 (93.3%)** |
| Rejected round 0, approved round 1 | 55 (4.4%) |
| Rejected both rounds (falls through) | 32 (2.6%) |
| check_convergence ever triggered | **0** |
| Early convergence (before max_iter) | **0** |

### The Prompt Bias

The supervisor prompt contains (convergence.py, line 142-143):

> **"YOUR DEFAULT SHOULD BE APPROVE. Only say REVIEW if you are confident the gaps you identify will materially improve the deliverable."**

This explicit default-to-approve instruction dominates model behavior.

### The Numbers Are Damning

Across the 1,167 tasks approved on first attempt:
- Average open signals at approval: **60.2** (range 0-118)
- Average critical open signals at approval: **6.2**

The supervisor approves with 60+ open signals because the prompt tells it to default to approve unless gaps are "material."

### The Non-Deterministic Verdict

55 tasks were rejected then approved with **identical state** — open signal count unchanged between rejection and approval. The verdict flipped from LLM non-determinism, not from any new work. Similarly, 32 tasks rejected twice had supervisor signals addressed by workers, but the second verdict was still rejection — again with unchanged state.

### Fix Potential

The supervisor review provides value in the ~7% of tasks where it rejects — it generates concrete, actionable signals that workers address in 3 extra iterations. But the approval verdict is essentially random noise on top of a 93% base-approve rate. Making the threshold stricter (removing the default-approve instruction) would likely improve performance by catching more omissions.

---

## 271. Convergence Is Dead Code in Practice

### Why check_convergence() Never Fires

Gate 2 in `check_convergence()`: "If ANY critical signal is open, return False immediately."

Critical signals accumulate throughout every run and are never fully resolved (§265: signals grow, never close). Many are questions about information that doesn't exist in the source documents. Gate 2 treats any open critical signal as a hard blocker.

**Result**: Across all 1,251 tasks in C23, the orchestrator never outputs `action: converge`. check_convergence() is never called. Every task exhausts max_iter=12.

### The Cascade

1. Workers generate unanswerable questions → marked as critical signals
2. Critical signals never resolve → Gate 2 always blocks
3. Convergence never triggers → every task runs 12 iterations
4. 12 iterations × 64x degradation after iter 1 → 60% of budget wasted (§261, §267)

### Fix

Either:
1. Expire critical signals after N iterations without resolution (currently infinite lifetime)
2. Distinguish between "critical and answerable" vs "critical and unanswerable" signals
3. Lower Gate 2 to only block on signals that have been explicitly addressed and re-opened

---

## 272. Output Format Analysis: Format Is Confound, Not Cause

### Pass Rates by Output Format (C23, 1,251 tasks)

| Format | Pass | Total | Rate | Avg Criteria |
|--------|------|-------|------|-------------|
| docx-single | 53 | 959 | **5.5%** | 78.6% |
| docx-multi (2 files) | 150 | 241 | **62.2%** | 96.6% |
| docx-multi (3 files) | 6 | 13 | 46.2% | 94.8% |
| docx-multi (4 files) | 2 | 4 | 50% | 99.1% |
| docx-multi (6+ files) | 0 | 6 | 0% | 93-99% |
| mixed docx+xlsx | 7 | 22 | 31.8% | 90% |
| xlsx-single | 3 | 4 | **75%** | 99.7% |

### The Confound

The 5.5% vs 62.2% gap between docx-single and docx-multi is NOT caused by format. It is caused by:
1. **Task difficulty correlation**: Multi-file tasks tend to have clearer, more decomposed rubrics
2. **Synthesis path difference**: Multi-file → file-scoped decomposition (§269)
3. **Criteria score ceiling**: Single-file tasks average 78.6% criteria (rarely reach 100%); multi-file average 96.6%

Among docx-single tasks that score 90%+ on criteria, 53/198 (26.8%) pass — those are the ones that reach exactly 100%.

### No .pptx or .csv Outputs

No .pptx outputs exist anywhere in cycle23. No .csv task outputs either. The system only produces .docx and .xlsx.

---

## 273. The xlsx Sheet: Prefix Problem

### The Writer Mechanism

`_write_xlsx()` in runner.py creates workbook tabs ONLY when lines match `^#{1,3}\s*Sheet:\s*(.+)$`. If synthesis writes `## Tab 1 - EBITDA Reconciliation` instead of `## Sheet: Tab 1 - EBITDA Reconciliation`, all content lands on the single default "Analysis" sheet.

### Impact

Tasks requiring multi-tab xlsx workbooks fail per-tab criteria when the synthesis doesn't use the `Sheet:` prefix. Example: `banking-finance/draft-credit-compliance-cert` (79/87 criteria) fails C-068 "XLSX has Tab 5: Fixed Charge Coverage Ratio Calculation" because the FCCR content went into the default sheet instead of its own tab.

### The Judge False Positive

One failing xlsx task (`employment-labor/extract-compliance-obligations`, 80/81) fails because the judge claims the output is "text-based format" rather than actual xlsx. The file IS a valid .xlsx with 79 tabs. The judge appears to evaluate extracted text content, sees Markdown syntax (`### `, `**bold**`), and concludes it's plain text.

This is a scorer artifact, not a system failure.

---

## 274. The File-Count Cliff: 6+ Files Always Fail

Tasks with 6+ output files achieve 0% pass rate despite 93-99% criteria scores. The binary scoring rule means that with N output files, each with M criteria, the probability of zero failures across all files drops exponentially.

At 98% per-criterion reliability:
- 1 file × 50 criteria: 0.98^50 = 36% expected pass
- 2 files × 50 criteria: 0.98^100 = 13% expected pass
- 6 files × 50 criteria: 0.98^300 = 0.2% expected pass

The system can't achieve 100% across 6+ files because even one missed criterion in any file kills the entire task. This is a mathematical ceiling that becomes dominant above 4 files.

---

## 275. Per-File Synthesis as Single-File Fix Template

### The Opportunity

The multi-file path (`synthesize_file_deliverables`) proves that focused, decomposed synthesis calls with per-file verification prevent silent content dropping and achieve 58% pass rate.

The single-file path (`synthesize_deliverable`) uses one monolithic call and achieves 5.5% pass rate.

### The Proposed Architecture

Apply file-scoped decomposition to single-file tasks by treating logical sections as virtual "files":

1. **Section planning**: Split must_include items into 3-5 logical sections (e.g., by document topic, by criterion cluster, by output section)
2. **Per-section evidence**: Load only section-relevant evidence per synthesis call
3. **Per-section draft**: max_tokens=16K per section (same as per-file path)
4. **Per-section verification**: Run `_verify_completeness()` against section-specific items
5. **Assembly**: Concatenate verified sections into single output document

This is exactly what `_sectioned_synthesis()` already does — but with one critical difference: the current sectioned path splits by curation-assigned section labels, not by logical output structure. Aligning sections with output structure (as the multi-file path does) would inherit the multi-file path's advantages.

### Evidence This Would Work

The 5 sectioned-synthesis passes (§239) all achieved 100% criteria with 60-124 sections. The sectioned architecture itself works — the bottleneck is curation selectivity (§253), not section synthesis quality.

---

### Harvey LAB — Ordered by Expected Impact × Confidence

| Pri | Fix | Layer | Impact | Confidence | Evidence |
|-----|-----|-------|--------|------------|---------|
| **0a** | Revert gap whitelist (restore "extract_more") | Extraction | +28pp | Very High | §228, §245 |
| **0b** | Revert C30 features | All | Remove -27pp | Very High | §155, §245 |
| **1** | Restore rubric scaffolding in synthesis | Synthesis | +5-15pp | High | §254, §258 |
| **2** | Activate shadow_judge_audit() | Curation | +5-10pp | High | §253, §255, §256 |
| **3** | Apply per-file decomposition to single-file tasks | Synthesis | +10-20pp | Medium-High | §269, §275 |
| **4** | Activate analytical_steering() | Extraction | +3-8pp | Medium | §255, §261 |
| **5** | Increase must_include from ~20 to ~50 | Curation | +3-5pp | Medium | §253 |
| **6** | Fix supervisor approval bias (remove default-approve) | Loop | +2-5pp | Medium | §270 |
| **7** | Expire unanswerable signals after N iterations | Loop | +1-3pp | Medium | §265, §271 |
| **8** | Add template placeholder post-processing | Synthesis | +1-2pp | High | §241 |
| **9** | Add xlsx Sheet: prefix enforcement in synthesis | Synthesis | +1-2pp | High | §273 |
| **10** | Fix xlsx judge false positives | Scoring | +2-3 tasks | High | §273 |
| **11** | Add verb-specific worker prompts | Extraction | +2-4pp | Medium | §242, §243 |
| **12** | Enable Relation Debt Sensor | Extraction | +2-5pp | Low | §255 |
| **13** | Enable Calculation Debt | Extraction | +2-4pp | Low | §255, §263 |
| **14** | Enable Blackboard Maintenance | Curation | +1-3pp | Low | §255 |

### Cumulative Estimate

- **Fixes 0a+0b**: Restore ~28% (baseline C27)
- **Add 1-3**: Reach ~50-60% (rubric + audit + decomposition)
- **Add 4-7**: Reach ~65-70% (extraction + loop fixes)
- **Add 8-14**: Reach ~70-75% (polish)
- **Beyond 75%**: Requires new architectural capabilities (cross-doc reasoning graph, multi-pass extraction, calculation verification)

---

## 277. Orchestrator Dispatch Logic: No Phase-Shifting

### What the Orchestrator Sees

The orchestrator receives a formatted prompt containing:
- Task instruction (raw task string)
- Per-document: name, read status, entry count, structural coverage %, extraction depth warnings if <70%
- State line: `iteration=N, entries={type_counts}, budget=X% used`
- Up to 15 open signals (critical + high only — medium/low are INVISIBLE)
- Up to 15 recent entries from CURRENT iteration only (200 chars each)
- Up to 10 disputed entries (status=disputed or confidence<0.4)

**The orchestrator does NOT see**: all historical entries, low/medium signals, or full document text.

### Output Schema

Two possible outputs:
1. **Workers**: 1-5 worker tasks, each with description, reads_from_blackboard, reads_from_documents, expected_output_type, priority, addresses_signals
2. **Converge**: action="converge" with reasoning and remaining_gaps

### No Phase-Shifting

**The orchestrator prompt is IDENTICAL on every iteration.** There is no explicit phase transition (read→analyze→synthesize). The only implicit phase-shifting comes from:
- Budget pressure: "Budget >70%? Focus on critical only"
- Signal state changes: as signals get addressed, new ones open
- Extraction depth warnings: if document coverage <70%

This means the orchestrator has no mechanism to transition from observation-heavy mode to analysis-heavy mode. It dispatches the same worker types in iteration 12 as iteration 1, explaining the 64x efficiency degradation (§261).

### Token Limits

| Call | max_tokens |
|------|-----------|
| Orchestrator | 4,096 |
| Worker (all types) | 8,192 |
| Direct analysis | 16,384 |
| Convergence check | 2,048 |
| Supervisor review | 4,096 |

### Signal Visibility Gap

Medium and low priority signals are INVISIBLE to the orchestrator. They expire after 3 iterations. This means:
- Workers generate observations that produce medium-priority insights
- The orchestrator never sees these insights
- They expire before anyone acts on them
- Only critical/high signals (mostly unanswerable questions) get attention

This creates a structural attention bias toward unanswerable questions and away from actionable analytical opportunities.

---

## 278. Document Reading Coverage: 100% Read, Failures Are Depth Not Breadth

### Coverage Numbers (C23, 1,251 tasks, 9,537 documents)

| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Documents fully read | 95.2% |
| Documents partially read | 4.8% |
| Documents skipped entirely | **0%** |
| Documents with extraction depth warning | 23% |

**Every document in every task is read.** The system never skips a document. The 4.8% "partially read" is misleading — it's caused by section name aliasing (workers read sections but use different names than the section index, so `mark_section_read()` can't match and clear them).

### The xlsx Section Indexing Bug

The section indexer classifies spreadsheet row content (e.g., "1.10 Corporate Organization") as level-3 sections via regex. Initial reading skips level >2, so xlsx files accumulate hundreds of "unread" rows permanently. This inflates section-unread counts without representing actual gaps.

81 tasks at "<70% section coverage" all have xlsx files with row-level sections. Ignoring xlsx rows, section coverage is effectively 100%.

### Coverage Does NOT Correlate with Task Outcome

| Section coverage | Tasks | Avg criteria% | All-pass rate |
|-----------------|-------|---------------|---------------|
| 100% | 892 | 83.3% | 17.9% |
| 90-99% | 146 | 83.0% | 20.5% |
| 70-89% | 132 | 85.2% | 18.9% |
| <70% (xlsx inflation) | 81 | 71.8% | 8.6% |

The <70% bucket's lower pass rate is caused by task type (spreadsheet-heavy calculation tasks), not missing coverage.

### What Actually Drives Failures

Failing tasks with 100% document coverage fail on:
- Specific value precision (wrong leverage threshold: 40% vs 45%)
- Missing calculations (data was read but weighted averages never computed)
- Synthesis failures (failing to combine clause modifications into combined-effect finding)

**Failures are analysis-depth problems, not reading-breadth problems.**

---

## 279. Token Usage: The Strongest Single Predictor of Task Pass Rate

### Token Quintile Analysis (C23)

| Quintile | Avg Tokens | Avg Criteria% | All-Pass Rate |
|----------|-----------|---------------|---------------|
| Q1 (lowest) | 1,032K | 77.6% | **8.4%** |
| Q2 | 1,241K | 80.1% | 8.8% |
| Q3 | 1,381K | 82.5% | 12.4% |
| Q4 | 1,539K | 83.2% | 16.4% |
| Q5 (highest) | 1,976K | **90.0%** | **42.6%** |

Tasks using >2M tokens pass at **5x the rate** of tasks using <1M tokens. The 3M-token tasks achieved 100% criteria.

### Why This Matters

This is likely a **confound, not a cause**: multi-file tasks (which use more tokens due to per-file synthesis) pass at higher rates. But it suggests that the system's token budget (3M) is not the ceiling — tasks that organically use more tokens do better, implying the system terminates too early or wastes tokens on empty iterations.

### Combined with §261

The system uses only ~23% of its 3M token budget on average. Of that 23%:
- 24% goes to productive iter-1 extraction
- 60% goes to diminishing-returns iters 2-11
- 16% goes to iter-12 synthesis trigger

Redirecting the 60% waste toward directed analytical work (via analytical_steering) or deeper extraction would likely close the gap between Q1 and Q5.

---

## 280. State Conversion Pipeline: Tag-Blind Curation Is the Bottleneck

### The Pipeline

```
Documents → Workers → Blackboard (500-1200 entries)
    ↓
Phase 7a: State Conversion Review → adds 10-30 analytical entries
    ↓
Phase 7b: Plan Coverage Review → adds coverage gap entries
    ↓
Phase 7c: State Repair → addresses repairable gaps (NOT in C23)
    ↓
Phase 8: Curation → selects must_include items from ALL entries
    ↓
Obligations → prepends 20-60 obligation dicts (higher priority)
    ↓
Synthesis → uses combined must_include + obligations
```

### The Critical Finding: Curation Is Tag-Blind

`_curate_cluster()` in curation.py passes all entries as plain text to the model. **There is no tag-based filtering, boosting, or special treatment for state_conversion entries.** The `state_conversion` tag is metadata that curation completely ignores.

This means state_conversion entries — the analytical enrichment the system worked to produce — compete on equal footing with raw observations for must_include slots. With 127+ entries per cluster, curation does significant lossy compression and may drop state_conversion entries.

### The Cross-Cutting Cluster Problem

When state_conversion creates cross-document analytical entries (e.g., "securing US leniency saves up to $175M"), they get `source=None` because they derive from multiple documents. These land in the `cross_cutting` cluster — the most crowded and least coherent cluster — where they compete against seed strategy entries, coverage gaps, and other analytical entries with no priority signal.

**10 of 30 SC entries** land in specific document clusters (single-source). **20 of 30 land in cross_cutting** — the worst place for must_include selection.

### State Conversion Is Not the Selectivity Bottleneck

State conversion adds valuable analytical entries, but the bottleneck is curation's inability to prioritize them. The fix is not more state conversion — it's tag-aware curation that boosts state_conversion, calculation, and strategy entries over raw observations.

---

## 281. The _select_repair_gaps() Whitelist: Why Most Gaps Are Unrepairable

### The Whitelist

```python
state_repairable_work = {"calculate", "compare", "legal_authority", "severity", "recommendation"}
```

### What It Excludes

`extract_more` and `unanswerable` — the two most common gap types. In a typical task:
- 8 of 10 plan_coverage gaps have `missing_work=extract_more` — **not repairable**
- Only 2 have `recommendation` or `compare` — **repairable**

The whitelist exists because state_repair cannot dispatch readers back to documents — it can only perform derivation work (math, comparison, citation). But `extract_more` is the dominant gap type because the swarm most often fails by not finding facts in documents.

### Why the C29 Regression Was Catastrophic

The C29 commit (0e2f6ba) narrowed this whitelist further. Before C29, `extract_more` was eligible for repair — meaning state_repair would at least ATTEMPT to derive missing content from existing entries. After C29, those gaps were silently dropped. Combined with removing rubric generation, this eliminated both the input-enrichment and repair pathways simultaneously.

---

## 282. Orchestrator Signal Blindness

### The Visibility Filter

| Priority | Visible to Orchestrator | Visible to Workers | Lifetime |
|----------|------------------------|-------------------|----------|
| critical | YES (up to 15) | YES | Infinite |
| high | YES (up to 15) | YES | Infinite |
| medium | **NO** | YES (if assigned) | 3 iterations |
| low | **NO** | YES (if assigned) | 3 iterations |

### Why This Matters

Workers generate observations that produce medium-priority insights. The orchestrator never sees these insights. They expire after 3 iterations before anyone acts on them.

Meanwhile, the orchestrator's attention is monopolized by critical/high signals — most of which are unanswerable questions (§265). The system's own analytical discoveries get auto-expired while unanswerable questions persist forever.

### The Interaction with §261 (64x Degradation)

Late iterations produce few new entries because:
1. The orchestrator only sees stale critical signals it already tried to address
2. It dispatches workers targeting those same signals
3. Workers can't answer unanswerable questions
4. No new productive work gets dispatched
5. Tokens are spent, nothing is produced

If medium-priority analytical insights were visible to the orchestrator, it could dispatch follow-up analytical work in later iterations instead of repeating futile attempts at unanswerable questions.

---

## 283. The Extraction Depth Check

### Mechanism

After initial reading, the system runs an extraction depth check comparing `len(entries from doc)` vs `doc.structural_profile["numbered_items"] * 0.5`. If coverage is below 50%, it fires a `convergence_gap` signal triggering re-extraction.

### Frequency

Fires in ~23% of tasks. This means nearly a quarter of tasks don't extract enough items from at least one document during initial reading. The re-extraction signal is the only automated mechanism for recovering from thin initial passes.

### Limitation

The check uses `numbered_items` from the structural profile as ground truth, but structural profiles are themselves model-generated estimates (from a 1,024-token call in Phase 2). When the profile underestimates numbered_items, the threshold is too low. When it overestimates, the check fires unnecessarily.

---

## 284. Parse Failures as Hard Losses

### The State Conversion Parse Failure Mode

When state_conversion's model call produces malformed JSON, zero entries are created. The retry logic splits into batches but in observed failure cases produced nothing.

Example: construction delay task (349 observation candidates, 278 analytical entries, parse_error=true, 0 entries created). The model couldn't fit 40 new entries into the response format, so nothing was added. This is an unrecoverable loss — neither curation nor obligations can manufacture the derived entries that state_conversion would have created.

### Impact

Parse failures affect ~5% of tasks. For those tasks, the entire analytical enrichment layer is missing — no cross-document calculations, no issue-recommendation bindings, no severity ratings. These tasks rely solely on raw observations for synthesis, which explains why some tasks with good extraction still fail at synthesis.

### Fix

More robust JSON parsing with partial recovery (accept the N entries that parsed successfully, discard malformed ones). The current approach is all-or-nothing.

---

---

## 285. Synthesis Obligations: Not the Gatekeeper

### What Obligations Are

An obligation is an LLM-reformulated "exact assertion the deliverable must make" — converting factual blackboard entries into judge-scorable claims. Types: `exact_value`, `legal_authority`, `cross_document_link`, `risk_recommendation`, `drafting_clause`, `output_structure`.

Generated from analytical entries (analysis + calculation + strategy + gap, up to 200K chars) + high-confidence sourced observations (up to 200 entries). Produces 20-60 obligation items per task.

### How They Combine with Must-Include

```
1. artifact_commitments    (insert(0), highest priority)  -- DISABLED in C23
2. obligations             (first into combined list)
3. curation items          (appended, deduped by first 60 chars of summary)
```

Dedup key = `summary[:60].lower().strip()`. Because obligations use different framing ("the deliverable must flag X") vs curation ("X per document Y"), 60-char prefixes rarely collide. Dedup is NOT a significant source of curation loss.

### Obligations Are NOT the Gatekeeper

For sectioned synthesis (73% of tasks, when combined list >40 items): every item gets its own synthesis chunk. No token-budget-driven loss possible. Obligations contribute ~3% of items for large tasks.

For flat synthesis (27%, ≤40 items): the combined total fits within a single 32K-token output call. No pressure either.

The real gatekeeper is curation — how many items each cluster produces and whether those items capture the critical facts.

---

## 286. MAJOR FINDING: The Phantom Entry Pattern

### Discovery

Comparing blackboard content between pass/fail task pairs in the same category reveals a critical failure mechanism: **facts from two separate documents co-exist in the blackboard but no analysis entry ever connects them**.

### Examples

**IP/Patent workstream (compare-matter-plan-against-engagement-letter, 55% criteria)**:
- Observation (CONF=1.0): "Scope includes eight workstreams: Due Diligence, SPA negotiation, Regulatory Analysis, **IP/Patent Review**, Real Estate Review..."
- The matter plan's workstreams are also extracted
- **ZERO analysis entry noting IP/Patent Review is absent from the matter plan**
- Scorer failed C-007 and C-008

**Cash netting cap (analyze-credit-agreement-markup, 41% criteria)**:
- Observation (CONF=1.0): "Cash Netting Cap: $25,000,000" (original agreement)
- Observation (CONF=1.0): "deduction cap of $50,000,000" (borrower markup)
- **ZERO analysis entry noting the $25M→$50M increase**
- Scorer failed C-013

**Retainer mechanism**:
- Observation: "retainer deposit is $150,000"
- Calculation: "Retainer credit of $50,000 applied to May/June/July invoices"
- **ZERO analysis entry comparing to EL's "apply against final invoices" mechanism**
- Scorer failed C-013 and C-014

### Frequency

Across 4 examined tasks:
- ~60% of all criterion failures trace to missing cross-reference analysis
- ~25% trace to section-level extraction coverage gaps
- ~15% trace to silent observation-level extraction misses

### Why Cross-References Never Form

1. Workers see only 300-char truncated summaries of context entries (§164)
2. Workers don't see the full entry graph or history
3. The prompt doesn't emphasize cross-referencing
4. `supports_entries` and `contradicts_entries` are NEVER populated (0/13,578 entries, §262)
5. No mechanism exists to present two related observations side-by-side to a worker

### Implication

This is the single most important structural failure in the system. The blackboard functions as a write-only log, not a connected knowledge graph. Facts accumulate in isolation. The analytical step that says "Document A says X, Document B says Y, therefore Z is a finding" never fires.

**This is exactly what analytical_steering() was designed to address** — it reads the full blackboard and dispatches targeted cross-document comparison tasks. It is dead code (§255).

---

## 287. Three Mechanisms of Content Failure (Entry-Level Analysis)

### Type 1: Missing Cross-Reference Analysis (60% of failures)

Facts from two documents co-exist in the blackboard but no analysis entry connects them. The worker that extracted Fact A never saw Fact B. The worker that extracted Fact B never saw Fact A. No worker was ever asked to compare them.

**Fix**: analytical_steering() + Relation Debt Sensor + cross-reference graph population

### Type 2: Section-Level Extraction Gap (25% of failures)

Specific document sections were not fully read or extracted. The blackboard has a gap entry acknowledging the miss, but the system can't backfill it.

Example: "Section 2.20 was not provided in the source text, preventing analysis of freebie baskets"

**Fix**: More thorough initial reading + re-extraction in later iterations + extraction depth check refinement

### Type 3: Silent Observation Miss (15% of failures)

The blackboard has NO awareness of missing content. A specific value or fact simply never appeared in any entry. No gap was flagged.

Example: "40% revolver threshold" appears zero times — the borrower's proposed change from 35% to 40% was never extracted from the markup.

**Fix**: More comprehensive extraction prompts + exhaustive enumeration requirements in worker dispatch

---

## 288. Plan Coverage Review: The System's Self-Awareness Mechanism

### How It Works

1. At seed time, the system commits to 5-15 specific questions and 5-10 completeness criteria
2. After all extraction and state conversion, the system interrogates its own blackboard adversarially
3. Each question/criterion gets evaluated: answered/partial/unanswered with `missing_work_type`
4. Missing work taxonomy: `extract_more`, `calculate`, `compare`, `legal_authority`, `severity`, `recommendation`, `unanswerable`

### The missing_work_type Distribution (C27, 247 gaps)

| Type | Count | Repairable? |
|------|-------|-------------|
| extract_more | **80** | NO |
| compare | 30 | YES |
| unanswerable | 14 | NO |
| recommendation | 6 | YES |
| severity | 5 | YES |
| calculate | 4 | YES |
| legal_authority | 2 | YES |

**`extract_more` is by far the largest gap type (58% of all gaps) and is explicitly excluded from repair.** The system knows what it missed but can't fix it.

### Reliability Evolution

| Cycle | Parse Success Rate |
|-------|-------------------|
| C13 (introduction) | 10% (5/48 tasks) |
| C27 (stable) | 98% (47/48 tasks) |

The 90% parse failure rate in C13 meant the feature contributed nothing. C27's 98% success rate enabled the +10pp improvement.

### Impact

- C23: +6.2pp from introducing coverage review (even with high parse error rate)
- C27: +10pp from stable coverage review + repair
- The repair step creates 87 entries across 48 tasks (68% repair success rate)
- All repairs are derivation work: calculations from existing data, comparisons between extracted facts, severity ratings for identified issues

---

## 289. The extract_more Gap: The Largest Unrepairable Failure Type

### The Problem

`extract_more` means "the information is in the source documents but wasn't captured in the blackboard." It is:
- The most common gap type (58% of all coverage gaps)
- Explicitly excluded from state repair (can't invent facts)
- The gap propagates to synthesis but synthesis has no re-extraction mechanism

### Why Repair Can't Address It

State repair's design constraint: "Do NOT write the final deliverable. Your only job is to add missing analytical state that existing downstream stages can use."

Repair can derive new conclusions from existing facts (calculate, compare, recommend). It cannot go back to source documents to extract new facts. This is architecturally correct — repair runs post-extraction and has no document access.

### What Would Fix It

1. **analytical_steering()** in the swarm loop — dispatches targeted extraction workers for specific facts
2. **A second extraction pass** post-coverage-review — re-read specific document sections that coverage review identified as missing
3. **Deeper initial reading** — extract more exhaustively in the first pass so fewer gaps exist

The system currently identifies 80 extract_more gaps per 48-task run and can fix zero of them.

---

## 290. Observation Verbosity vs Analysis Precision

### The Counterintuitive Finding

Failing tasks write LONGER analysis entries than passing tasks:

| Group | Avg Analysis Entry Length | Avg Strategy Entry Length |
|-------|-------------------------|-------------------------|
| PASS | 161 chars | 183 chars |
| FAIL | 282 chars | 194 chars |

### Why Length Hurts

Failing tasks produce verbose entries that contextualize and explain findings but miss the granular specifics the scorer cares about.

**Failing analysis entry** (282 chars): "The Engagement Letter scope is strictly limited to 'Phase 1: Preliminary Due Diligence and Regulatory Filing', whereas the Matter Plan includes 'Phase 2: Litigation Support and Discovery', which constitutes unauthorized scope creep."

**Passing analysis entry** (161 chars): "The Term Loan B interest rate in the Draft Credit Agreement is Adjusted Term SOFR + 4.25%, which deviates from the Term Sheet rate of Adjusted Term SOFR + 4.00%."

The passing entry is shorter but directly states the specific deviation with exact numbers. The failing entry describes the general issue but misses the precise fact the scorer tests for.

### Implication

Entry quality should be measured by **specificity** (includes exact values, section numbers, party names) not **verbosity** (explains context, motivation, implications). Workers should be prompted to produce shorter, more factually dense entries rather than contextual analysis.

---

## 291. Strategy Entry Grounding: Source Attribution Predicts Outcome

### The Data

| Group | Strategy Entries | With Source Attribution |
|-------|-----------------|----------------------|
| PASS (draft-nda-markup) | 69 | **73%** |
| FAIL (analyze-credit-markup) | 30 | 47% |
| FAIL (compare-matter-plan) | 21 | 24% |

### Why Grounding Matters

Source-grounded strategy entries are specific and actionable:

> "We must demand the complete deletion of the $5,000,000 liquidated damages provision in Section 7.2. This is a firm walk-away red line." (CONF=0.98, source=draft-nda-theranova.docx)

Ungrounded strategy entries are generic remediation templates:

> "To resolve the $100,000 Fee Cap discrepancy, the Relationship Partner must either: (a) draft a formal amendment..." (CONF=0.98, NO_SOURCE)

The first is directly actionable by synthesis. The second prescribes remediation for a finding that may or may not be correct — it's a hypothesis, not a grounded instruction.

### Implication

Strategy entries without source attribution are noise. Workers should be required to cite source documents for strategy entries, or strategy entries should be deprioritized when ungrounded.

---

## 292. Complete System Failure Model (Final, Post-§285-291)

### The Four Failure Layers

| Layer | % of Failures | Root Cause | Primary Fix |
|-------|--------------|------------|-------------|
| **1. Cross-Reference Gap** | **35%** | Facts exist in isolation, never connected | analytical_steering(), Relation Debt |
| **2. Extraction Depth** | **25%** | Facts not extracted from source docs | Deeper initial reading, re-extraction pass |
| **3. Curation Selectivity** | **25%** | Facts in blackboard but not in must_include | shadow_judge_audit(), tag-aware curation |
| **4. Synthesis Quality** | **15%** | Must-include items dropped or distorted | Rubric scaffolding, per-file decomposition |

### The Fix Sequence

1. **Revert C29/C30 regressions** (0a/0b): Restore 28% baseline
2. **Activate analytical_steering()**: Address Layer 1 (35% of failures)
3. **Activate shadow_judge_audit()**: Address Layer 3 (25% of failures)
4. **Restore rubric scaffolding**: Address Layer 4 (15% of failures)
5. **Apply per-file decomposition to single-file tasks**: Address Layer 4 further
6. **Enable Relation Debt Sensor**: Address Layer 1 further (populate cross-reference graph)
7. **Increase must_include + tag-aware curation**: Address Layer 3 further
8. **Add re-extraction pass post-coverage-review**: Address Layer 2

### Estimated Trajectory

| Fixes Applied | Estimated Pass Rate |
|--------------|-------------------|
| None (current C30) | 0% |
| 0a+0b (revert) | ~28% |
| + analytical_steering + shadow_judge | ~45-55% |
| + rubric scaffolding + decomposition | ~60-70% |
| + debt sensors + curation fix | ~70-75% |
| + re-extraction + all dead code | ~75-85% |
| Beyond 85% | Requires architectural innovation |

---

---

## 293. CRITICAL FINDING: Model Routing Mismatch — analytical_caller Never Wired

### The Intended Design

`worker_dispatch.py` (lines 373-377) contains logic to route workers with `expected_output_type ∈ {analysis, calculation, strategy}` to an `analytical_caller` (smarter model). This is the designed model escalation for complex reasoning tasks.

### The Reality

**`analytical_caller` is NEVER passed.** Every call to `execute_workers_parallel` in `__init__.py` (lines 237 and 301) passes only `(tasks_list, blackboard, caller)` — the `analytical_caller` keyword argument defaults to `None`.

### The Model Map (C23)

| Phase | Caller | Model | Impact |
|-------|--------|-------|--------|
| Structural profiling | caller | gemini-3.1-flash-lite | Low |
| Initial reading | caller | gemini-3.1-flash-lite | Low |
| **Orchestrator** | **caller** | **gemini-3.1-flash-lite** | **HIGH — dispatches all work** |
| **ALL workers (observation, analysis, calculation, strategy)** | **caller** | **gemini-3.1-flash-lite** | **CRITICAL — cross-ref, legal reasoning, calculations all on weak model** |
| Curation | caller | gemini-3.1-flash-lite | HIGH — selects must_include |
| Completeness verification | caller | gemini-3.1-flash-lite | Medium |
| Seed planning | review_caller | gemini-3.5-flash | Correct |
| Direct analysis | review_caller | gemini-3.5-flash | Correct |
| Supervisor review | review_caller | gemini-3.5-flash | Correct |
| State conversion | review_caller | gemini-3.5-flash | Correct |
| Plan coverage | review_caller | gemini-3.5-flash | Correct |
| Obligations | review_caller | gemini-3.5-flash | Correct |
| Synthesis | synth_caller | gemini-3.5-flash | Correct |

### Why This Is Critical

Cross-reference analysis, legal reasoning, severity ratings, and calculations — the five failure categories blocking 90%+ (§242, §243) — are ALL handled by gemini-3.1-flash-lite. The smarter model (gemini-3.5-flash) only sees the work AFTER extraction, in reviewer/supervisor phases.

### The Fix

One line: pass `analytical_caller=review_caller` in every `execute_workers_parallel` call in `__init__.py` (lines 237 and 301). This activates the already-built routing logic that routes analysis/calculation/strategy workers to the smarter model.

### Expected Impact

- Analysis workers on gemini-3.5-flash would produce higher-quality cross-references
- Calculation workers on gemini-3.5-flash would produce more accurate arithmetic
- Strategy workers on gemini-3.5-flash would produce more grounded strategies
- Estimated: +3-8pp on tasks requiring analytical depth (identify, extract, compare verbs)

---

## 294. Direct Analysis: The Only Cross-Reference Mechanism

### What run_direct_analysis() Does

A single post-loop LLM call using the review_caller (gemini-3.5-flash) at 16,384 max tokens — 2x the worker budget. It reads the entire blackboard (entries truncated to 400 chars) and produces 13-26 analytical entries.

### Why It's Special

Direct analysis entries are the **ONLY entries in the entire system with `supports` populated**. Every DA entry has 2-15 `supports` IDs, creating actual cross-reference links. Worker entries have 0/13,578 cross-references (§262).

### Limitations

1. **Runs once, post-loop**: Too late to inform further extraction — can only connect facts already on the blackboard
2. **Top-K, not exhaustive**: Produces 10-30 outputs from a blackboard of 500-1200 entries — can't systematically enumerate all cross-document comparisons
3. **Can't reach back into documents**: Works from 400-char summaries, not source text — can't fill extraction gaps it notices

### Comparison with analytical_steering (Dead Code)

| Property | run_direct_analysis | analytical_steering (dead) |
|----------|-------------------|--------------------------|
| When | Once, after iter 12 | Every 4th iteration |
| Model | gemini-3.5-flash | gemini-3.5-flash |
| Max tokens | 16,384 | 4,096 |
| Output | Direct entries with supports | Worker task descriptions (indirect) |
| Document access | No (400-char summaries) | Yes (workers can re-read docs) |
| Cross-references | Yes (2-15 per entry) | Indirect (workers would need to populate) |

**The right design is BOTH**: analytical_steering every 4th iteration (to surface gaps while documents can still be re-read) and direct_analysis post-loop (for final synthesis-level connections).

---

## 295. The Curation Model Mismatch

### The Finding

Curation — which selects must_include items from the blackboard — runs on **gemini-3.1-flash-lite** (the weaker model). This is the gatekeeper step that determines what synthesis sees (§253, §280).

### Why This Matters

Curation selects ~20 items from 500-1200 entries — a 97% discard rate. The selection model must:
- Evaluate relative importance of facts across diverse domains
- Distinguish synthesis-relevant content from noise
- Identify low-salience-but-critical facts (e.g., TDEC site listing, §253)

These are judgment-intensive tasks suited to the smarter model. Using the weaker model for this gatekeeper step means the most consequential selection in the pipeline is made by the least capable model.

### The Fix

Route curation to `review_caller` (gemini-3.5-flash). Since curation runs once per task, the cost increase is negligible (~1 additional gemini-3.5-flash call per task).

---

## 296. Cross-Benchmark Failure Pattern Convergence

### Common Failure Modes Across Benchmarks

| Failure Mode | Harvey LAB | OfficeQA Pro | AMA-Bench |
|-------------|-----------|-------------|-----------|
| Missing content (extraction) | 38% of criterion failures | 83.5% (numeric_mismatch) | Not tested |
| Cross-document reasoning | 35% (§286 phantom entries) | 7.7% pass rate at 3+ docs | N/A (single-doc) |
| Calculation accuracy | 7% of criterion failures | 100% of numeric failures | N/A |
| Format compliance | 4-7% of criterion failures | 16.5% (list format) | N/A |

### The Universal Bottleneck

Across all benchmarks, the primary failure is **the system reads the data but doesn't accurately process it**:
- Harvey LAB: Facts are extracted but not connected (phantom entries) or not curated (selectivity gap)
- OfficeQA: Numbers are read but arithmetic is wrong (31 sum failures on single documents)
- Both: Multi-document aggregation fails systematically

### Why 20-25% Is the Convergence Point

The system's per-item reliability of ~85% combines with:
- Binary scoring (Harvey LAB): 0.85^59 ≈ 0.01% expected, observed 18% due to criteria clustering
- Strict numeric tolerance (OfficeQA): 0.1% tolerance kills near-miss calculations
- Multi-doc complexity scaling: pass rate drops from 37% (1 doc) to 0% (4+ docs)

All three effects independently push toward a 20-25% ceiling.

---

## 297. The Complete Architecture Map

### Phase Sequence

```
Phase 1: Structural Profiling (gemini-3.1-flash-lite)
    ↓
Phase 2: Seed Planning (gemini-3.5-flash)
    ↓
Phase 3: Initial Reading (gemini-3.1-flash-lite, parallel)
    ↓
Phase 4: Swarm Loop × 12 iterations
    ├── Orchestrator dispatch (gemini-3.1-flash-lite)
    ├── Workers × 5 parallel (gemini-3.1-flash-lite — ALL types)
    ├── Signal prioritization (gemini-3.1-flash-lite)
    └── Convergence check (gemini-3.1-flash-lite — never fires)
    ↓
Phase 5: Direct Analysis (gemini-3.5-flash, 16K tokens, once)
    ↓
Phase 6: Supervisor Review × 2 rounds (gemini-3.5-flash)
    ├── If rejected: 3 extra worker iterations (gemini-3.1-flash-lite)
    ↓
Phase 7a: State Conversion Review (gemini-3.5-flash)
Phase 7b: Plan Coverage Review (gemini-3.5-flash)
Phase 7c: Plan Coverage State Repair (gemini-3.5-flash)
    ↓
Phase 8: Curation (gemini-3.1-flash-lite — MISMATCH)
    ↓
Phase 9: Synthesis Obligations (gemini-3.5-flash)
    ↓
Phase 10: Synthesis (gemini-3.5-flash)
    ├── Sectioned: parallel sections × 8K tokens each
    ├── File-scoped: per-file × 16K tokens each
    └── Flat: single call × 32K tokens
    ↓
Phase 11: Verify + Augment (gemini-3.1-flash-lite for verify, gemini-3.5-flash for augment)
    ↓
Phase 12: Output Writing (docx/xlsx conversion)
```

### Model Usage Summary

| Model | Calls per task (typical) | Purpose |
|-------|------------------------|---------|
| gemini-3.1-flash-lite | 70-75 | Workers, orchestrator, curation, verification |
| gemini-3.5-flash | 26-73 | Seed, direct analysis, supervisor, state conversion, obligations, synthesis |

### Key Mismatches

1. **Workers**: ALL worker types (including analysis, calculation, strategy) on weak model — intended routing to strong model never wired
2. **Curation**: Gatekeeper step on weak model — determines what synthesis sees
3. **Orchestrator**: Dispatch decisions on weak model — determines what work gets done

---
