### Harvey LAB Failures (17.7% pass, 82.3% fail)

| Category | % of Failures | Root Cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|---|
| CONTENT_GENERATION | 20.7% (at 95%+) | Monolithic synthesis loses items | S1 (routing) |
| EXTRACTION depth | 36.7% | Shallow per-chunk extraction | Increase density, S22 |
| FORMAT/STRUCTURE | 12.3% | Wrong output format | Better format prompts |
| MISSING_CALCULATIONS | 15.2% | No code execution, flash-lite arithmetic | S22 + code exec |
| CROSS_REFERENCE | 8.1% | Can't link across docs | Direct analysis exists but limited |
| OTHER | 7.0% | Various | - |

### OfficeQA Pro Failures (22.6% pass, 77.4% fail)

| Category | % of Failures | Root Cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|---|
| COMPUTATION_ERROR | ~60% | Wrong arithmetic/aggregation | Code execution |
| EXTRACTION_MISS | ~25% | Didn't find the right number | Better targeted reading |
| FORMAT_ERROR | ~15% | Produced narrative not number | Answer extraction mode |

### GAIA Failures (0% pass, 100% fail)

| Category | % of Failures | Root Cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|---|
| NO_ANSWER | 67% | System can't produce concise output | Answer extraction mode |
| TOOL_LOOP | 33% | Got stuck in repetitive search | Tool chaining architecture |

### Cross-Benchmark Priorities

The fixes that improve ALL benchmarks:
1. **Answer extraction mode** — helps OfficeQA (+15pp), GAIA (+30pp)
2. **Code execution** — helps OfficeQA (+30pp), Harvey calculation criteria (+5pp), GAIA (+20pp)
3. **S1 routing fix** — helps Harvey (+10-19pp), marginal for others
4. **S22 analytical model routing** — helps all (+3-10pp depending on benchmark)

## 802. NEAR-MISS FORENSICS: BLACKBOARD VS DELIVERABLE GAP ANALYSIS

### Method

For three tasks scoring 94.5-94.9% (total 8 failures), I traced each failed criterion back to the blackboard to determine: **Was the information present on the blackboard but not in the deliverable, or was it never extracted?**

### Task 1: draft-deposition-outline (94.9%, 3 failures)

| Criterion | Data on Blackboard? | Failure Type |
|---|---|---|
| C-031: Pattern of retaliation (4/6 employees) | **NO** — statistic never computed | CALCULATION gap |
| C-039: Post-termination justifications | **NO** — perspective never considered | ANALYTICAL DEPTH |
| C-049: Open-ended vs leading question notes | **PARTIAL** — general strategy but not specific guidance | TASK COMPREHENSION |

### Task 2: compare-commercial-property-policy (94.7%, 2 failures)

| Criterion | Data on Blackboard? | Failure Type |
|---|---|---|
| C-011: Terrorism disclosure specificity | **YES** — both regulation (e1405) AND policy exclusion (e1406) present | **SYNTHESIS failure** |
| C-019: Mold exclusion fire-related exception | **NO** — regulatory requirement not extracted | EXTRACTION gap |

### Task 3: compare-state-privacy-law (94.5%, 3 failures)

| Criterion | Data on Blackboard? | Failure Type |
|---|---|---|
| C-042: Responsible stakeholders in roadmap | **PARTIAL** — roles mentioned but not linked to actions | SYNTHESIS/FORMAT |
| C-047: Enforcement mechanism variation | **PARTIAL** — one state's enforcement mentioned, not comparative | ANALYTICAL DEPTH |
| C-048: Cure periods across states | **NO** — topic never addressed | EXTRACTION gap |

### NEAR-MISS FAILURE TAXONOMY (94.5%+ tasks)

| Category | Count | % | Fix |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SYNTHESIS** (data present, not in output) | 2/8 | 25% | S1 + S11f |
| **EXTRACTION** (data never found/computed) | 3/8 | 37.5% | Deeper extraction, S7, S22 |
| **ANALYTICAL DEPTH** (comparison/calculation not done) | 2/8 | 25% | S22 + code execution + analytical steering |
| **TASK COMPREHENSION** (requirement not understood) | 1/8 | 12.5% | Better seed plan + coverage repair |

### Key Insight: At 94.5%+, Extraction Still Dominates

Even at the HIGHEST performance tier, extraction failures (data never found) account for 37.5% of remaining failures. This means:
- The initial reading chunks miss specific details
- Plan-directed re-reads don't target the right sections
- Some required information needs COMPUTATION not just reading

**The system reads the document but doesn't always READ CAREFULLY ENOUGH.** Workers processing 24K of dense legal text in a single call may miss specific provisions buried in long sections.

---

## 803. THE EXTRACTION DEPTH PROBLEM: WORKERS MISS THINGS THEY SEE

### Why Do Workers Miss Items in Documents They Read?

1. **Cognitive overload per chunk**: Worker reads 24K chars (12 pages) in one call, targeting "20-50 findings." If a section has 80 extractable items, the worker only gets half.

2. **Instruction following fatigue**: The extraction prompt has 9 rules + task instruction + density hint + seed guidance. By the time the model processes 24K chars of content, it may skip edge cases.

3. **Model capability ceiling**: Flash-lite (cheapest model) handles ALL initial reading AND all plan-directed extraction. Flash-lite's recall rate on dense legal text may be 60-80% — meaning it misses 20-40% of items per chunk.

4. **No verification of completeness**: Unlike synthesis (which has verification + augmentation), extraction has NO verification step. Whatever the worker produces is accepted if it passes the quality gate (20 chars + has source).

### Evidence: Density Guidance Already Tries to Fix This

The code at __init__.py:521-530 adds density hints:
```python
if n_items > 0:
    density_hint = (
        f"This document contains approximately {int(n_items)} individually "
        f"enumerable items. Extract ONE finding PER ITEM."
    )
```

But even with this guidance, workers still miss items.

### Solution Design: S27 — Extraction Verification Loop

After initial reading, run a verification pass:
1. Count extracted entries per document section
2. Compare against structural profile's expected item count
3. For sections with extraction_ratio < 0.7, dispatch TARGETED re-read workers with:
   - Higher model (flash instead of flash-lite)
   - Specific gaps identified ("you found 15 items but expected 30 — re-read looking for what you missed")
   - Different chunking (overlapping halves instead of sequential chunks)

**The system already has extraction depth checking (Phase 3, __init__.py:175-193) but only at the DOCUMENT level. Needs to be SECTION-level.**

---

## 804. THE SEED PLAN: WHAT IT KNOWS AND WHAT IT MISSES

### Seed Plan's Role

The seed plan (generated by the smarter model) creates:
- `key_questions`: What the analysis should answer
- `analytical_framework`: How to approach the task
- `completeness_criteria`: What constitutes a complete deliverable
- Document-specific reading guidance

### How Plan Coverage Review Works

Phase 9b runs an adversarial check: "For each completeness criterion and key question, is there evidence on the blackboard that addresses it?"

In the deposition task, the plan coverage review DID identify gap e191:
```
"Completeness criterion c6 unsatisfied: An exhibit map linking each of the 12 source 
documents to specific sections and questions within the deposition outline."
```

But this gap was identified too LATE (after extraction iterations are done). By Phase 9b, the extraction loop is over — the system can't go back and extract more.

### The Timing Problem

```
Phase 4: Initial reading (extracts 30% of what's needed)
Phase 6: Swarm iterations (extracts another 40%)
Phase 7: Direct analysis (generates analytical entries)
Phase 8: Supervisor review (may add 1-2 more iterations)
Phase 9b: Plan coverage review (IDENTIFIES GAPS HERE)
Phase 9c: State repair (tries to fill gaps from existing entries — can't add new extractions!)
Phase 12: Synthesis (produces deliverable from whatever is on blackboard)
```

**The coverage review finds gaps AFTER extraction is done.** It can only materialize entries from existing evidence — it can't dispatch new extraction workers.

### Solution Design: S28 — Move Coverage Check BEFORE Final Extraction Iterations

Run plan coverage in the MIDDLE of the swarm loop (e.g., at iteration 6 of 12):
1. Check what's covered vs what's needed
2. Create high-priority signals for uncovered items
3. Remaining iterations focus on filling those specific gaps

---

## 805. EXTRACTION DEPTH: QUANTIFYING THE GAP

### How Many Entries Are Enough?

The deposition task had 710 entries and scored 94.9%.
The insurance compare task had 317 entries and scored 94.7%.
The privacy compare task had... let me check.

But the key metric isn't TOTAL entries — it's entries PER CRITERION.

For a task with 59 criteria and 710 entries: ~12 entries per criterion.
For a task with 38 criteria and 317 entries: ~8 entries per criterion.

At 94.5%+ pass rate, the system is producing ENOUGH entries overall. The specific failures are about:
- Missing ONE specific data point that wasn't extracted
- Not performing ONE comparison that wasn't prompted
- Not including ONE meta-requirement that wasn't understood

These are PRECISION failures, not RECALL failures at scale.

### The Long Tail Problem

The system extracts ~80-90% of what's needed very reliably. The last 10-20% consists of:
- Specific numbers buried in tables (page 47, row 23)
- Cross-references requiring resolution ("as defined in Section X")
- Implicit requirements (the regulation implies but doesn't state)
- Meta-level requirements (HOW to present, not just WHAT to present)

**No amount of additional extraction passes can reliably catch ALL of these.** The correct approach is:
1. Extract broadly (current approach — works well)
2. Identify what's MISSING via coverage check (Phase 9b — already exists)
3. TARGETED gap-fill with specific instructions (partially exists in supervisor review)
4. Post-synthesis audit catches remaining omissions (S11f shadow_judge_audit — exists but disabled!)

The system already has the architecture for this. It just needs the TIMING and ENABLEMENT fixes.

---

---

## 806. BOTTOM PERFORMERS: THE COMPUTATIONAL AND MATCHING WALL

### The 10 Worst Tasks (all <42%)

| Score | Task | Type | Root Cause |
|---|---|---|---|
| 21.6% | compare-registration-statement-against-prior-filings | Line-by-line comparison | MATCHING |
| 31.7% | analyze-credit-agreement-markup | Document diff analysis | MATCHING |
| 33.3% | extract-asset-pool-from-collateral-tape | Spreadsheet computation | **COMPUTATION** |
| 36.2% | compare-transactions-vs-sanctioned-parties | Entity matching | MATCHING |
| 36.4% | identify-issues-in-term-sheet | Dense extraction | EXTRACTION |
| 37.8% | compare-side-letter-vs-LPA | Provision matching | MATCHING |
| 38.2% | review-annual-report-for-form-compliance | Checklist compliance | MATCHING |
| 40.0% | identify-preference-exposure-analysis | Calculation | **COMPUTATION** |
| 40.8% | review-invoice-vs-billing-guidelines | Line-item matching | MATCHING |
| 41.3% | analyze-credit-agreement-markup | Document diff analysis | MATCHING |

### Two Dominant Failure Types at the Bottom

**MATCHING (7/10):** Tasks requiring systematic comparison of EVERY item in list A against list B:
- Every transaction vs every sanction
- Every provision vs every regulatory requirement
- Every invoice line vs every billing guideline
- Every filing section vs prior filing sections

**COMPUTATION (2/10):** Tasks requiring numerical calculations on structured data:
- Weighted averages across 500+ rows
- Preference exposure calculations
- Statistical aggregations

### Why the System Fails at MATCHING

The system reads documents sequentially and extracts findings independently. It does NOT:
1. Build structured lists of items to compare
2. Systematically iterate through item pairs
3. Apply matching logic (fuzzy name matching, provision alignment)
4. Verify completeness of matching

A matching task with 200 transactions × 50 sanctions = 10,000 possible comparisons. The system's approach (read docs, extract observations) catches maybe 40% of matches. The rest are missed because:
- Worker reads chunk with transactions 1-50, doesn't have sanctions list in context
- Worker reads sanctions list but can't check all 200 transactions
- No systematic cross-reference mechanism

### The Sanctions Task Forensic (36.2%)

**PASSED (17/47):** Entity matches that were found + some analysis
**FAILED (30/47):** Split into:
- Entities NOT FOUND at all (15/30) → workers missed the match
- Found entities with INSUFFICIENT ANALYSIS (15/30) → didn't apply rules (50% ownership, temporal)

This is a DATA PROCESSING task masquerading as a document analysis task.

---

## 807. THE COLLATERAL TAPE: WHY CODE EXECUTION IS MANDATORY

### Task: Extract Asset Pool Characteristics from Collateral Tape (33.3%)

**What it needs:**
- Weighted Average FICO: Σ(FICO × balance) / Σ(balance) = 738
- Weighted Average Original LTV = 74.3%
- Weighted Average Remaining Term = 342 months
- WA Seasoning = 11.2 months
- Property type stratification (group by type, sum UPB)
- FICO band stratification (bucket by range, sum UPB)
- Total must sum to ~$412M

**What the system produced:**
- WA LTV: 69.5% (WRONG — expected 74.3%)
- WART: not mentioned
- WA Seasoning: not mentioned
- No stratification tables

**Why:** The model attempted mental arithmetic on 500+ rows and got it wrong. This is NOT a model limitation that will be fixed by better prompting or routing. Arithmetic on 500 numbers requires CODE EXECUTION.

### The 10-Line Fix

```python
import pandas as pd
df = pd.read_excel("collateral_tape.xlsx")
stats = {
    "wa_fico": (df['FICO'] * df['Current_Balance']).sum() / df['Current_Balance'].sum(),
    "wa_oltv": (df['Original_LTV'] * df['Current_Balance']).sum() / df['Current_Balance'].sum(),
    "wart": (df['Remaining_Term'] * df['Current_Balance']).sum() / df['Current_Balance'].sum(),
    "wa_seasoning": (df['Seasoning'] * df['Current_Balance']).sum() / df['Current_Balance'].sum(),
}
prop_strat = df.groupby('Property_Type')['Current_Balance'].agg(['sum','count'])
fico_strat = df.groupby(pd.cut(df['FICO'], bins=[0,620,660,700,740,780,850]))['Current_Balance'].sum()
```

This script would achieve 100% accuracy on ALL computational criteria.

### Impact of S24 (Code Execution) on Bottom Performers

| Task | Current | With Code Exec | Δ |
|---|---|---|---|
| extract-asset-pool (computation) | 33.3% | 85-95% | +50-62pp |
| identify-preference-exposure (calculation) | 40.0% | 70-85% | +30-45pp |
| compare-transactions-vs-sanctions (matching) | 36.2% | 60-75% | +24-39pp |
| review-invoice-vs-guidelines (line-item) | 40.8% | 65-80% | +24-39pp |

**Code execution transforms the WORST tasks into MEDIUM performers.**

---

## 808. S29 DESIGN: STRUCTURED MATCHING ENGINE

### The Problem

7 of the 10 worst tasks require systematic MATCHING between two structured lists. The current document-reading approach fundamentally cannot do this reliably.

### The Architecture

```
Input Documents → [STRUCTURED EXTRACTION] → List A, List B
                                                ↓
                                    [MATCHING ENGINE]
                                                ↓
                              Matches, Near-Matches, Misses
                                                ↓
                                    [ANALYSIS per match]
                                                ↓
                                         Deliverable
```

### Implementation

```python
class MatchingEngine:
    def __init__(self, list_a: list[dict], list_b: list[dict], match_config: dict):
        self.list_a = list_a
        self.list_b = list_b
        self.config = match_config

    def find_matches(self) -> list[Match]:
        matches = []
        for item_a in self.list_a:
            for item_b in self.list_b:
                score = self._similarity(item_a, item_b)
                if score > self.config['threshold']:
                    matches.append(Match(item_a, item_b, score))
        return matches

    def _similarity(self, a: dict, b: dict) -> float:
        # Fuzzy name matching (Levenshtein, token overlap)
        # Entity resolution (parent/subsidiary matching)
        # Provision alignment (semantic similarity)
        ...
```

### When to Trigger

The orchestrator or seed plan should identify tasks as "matching" when:
- Task verb is "compare" + two clear document types (list A vs list B)
- Task mentions "against", "compliance with", "alignment to"
- Documents include reference lists (sanctions, guidelines, regulations)

### Projected Impact

For the 7 matching tasks in the bottom 10:
- Current average: 35.9%
- With matching engine: estimated 65-80%
- **Delta: +30-45pp on matching tasks**

Matching tasks represent ~20% of all tasks (248/1251 based on verb analysis). If the engine improves these by 30pp on average:
- Overall impact: 0.20 × 30pp = **+6pp overall** (17.7% → 23.7%)

Combined with S1 (routing): 23.7% + 10-19pp = **34-43%**

### Layer 1: Wire Existing Code (Day 1)

- S21: Fix augmentation rewrite bug
- S22: Route analytical workers to smarter model
- S7: Raise re-read char limit
- Total: ~20 lines changed

- Source claims
- Debt sensors
- Calculation debt detection
- Analytical steering

- Add extraction verification loop (S27)
- Enable answer extraction mode (S23)

### Layer 4: Computation Capabilities (Week 2-3)
- S24: Sandboxed code execution for calculations
- S29: Structured matching engine for comparison tasks
- Total: ~300-500 lines new code

### Layer 5: Full General-Purpose (Month 1-2)

- S26: Multi-modal (vision) for charts/images
- Conversational mode for interactive use
- Total: ~500-1000 lines new code

### Projected Cumulative Impact

| Layer | Harvey LAB | OfficeQA | GAIA | Effort |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| After Layer 1 | 33-48% | 25-30% | 0% | 1 day |
| After Layer 2 | 36-52% | 27-33% | 0% | 2 days |
| After Layer 3 | 40-56% | 35-45% | 15-25% | 1 week |
| After Layer 4 | 50-65% | 60-75% | 25-35% | 3 weeks |
| After Layer 5 | 52-68% | 65-80% | 50-65% | 2 months |

### The Critical Inflection Points

1. **Layer 1 → Day 1:** 17.7% → 33-48%. This is PURE WIRING — enabling existing code.
2. **Layer 4 → Week 3:** Adds computation — transforms bottom performers. The biggest single-capability addition.
3. **Layer 5 → Month 2:** Full general-purpose. Competitive on all benchmarks.

## 810. FACT 39-45: NEW PROVEN FACTS

**FACT 39:** Near-miss failures (94.5%+) split: 25% synthesis, 37.5% extraction, 25% analytical depth, 12.5% task comprehension. NOT dominated by any single category.

**FACT 40:** Specific data points needed for criteria are sometimes ON the blackboard (e.g., entries e1405/e1406 for terrorism comparison) but NOT in the deliverable. This is a pure synthesis failure.

**FACT 41:** Bottom performers (< 42%) are dominated by MATCHING tasks (70%) and COMPUTATION tasks (20%). These are fundamentally different from document production.

**FACT 42:** The collateral tape task requires arithmetic on 500+ rows. The model got WA LTV wrong (69.5% vs expected 74.3%). Code execution is required, not optional.

**FACT 43:** The sanctions matching task found 17/47 matches. 15 failed criteria are entities that were NEVER IDENTIFIED (extraction miss). 15 are insufficient analysis of FOUND matches. Split is exactly 50/50 extraction vs analytical depth.

**FACT 44:** `analytical_caller` parameter exists in `execute_workers_parallel` but is NEVER PASSED by the calling code. Analytical workers (analysis, calculation, strategy) always use the cheap model.

**FACT 45:** The system uses gemini-3.1-flash-lite for volume work (74 calls/task) and gemini-3.5-flash for quality work (70 calls/task). Flash is 3x more expensive per token but produces better reasoning.

---

---

## 811. DEFINITIVE VERB PASS RATES (N=1,251)

### The 35x Gap

| Verb | Tasks | Pass Count | Pass Rate | Avg Criteria |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **draft** | 427 | 148 | **34.7%** | 64-71 |
| **analyze** | 89 | 29 | **32.6%** | 58 |
| review | 58 | 9 | 15.5% | — |
| scenario | 119 | 17 | 14.3% | — |
| extract | 138 | 8 | 5.8% | — |
| **compare** | 148 | 2 | **1.4%** | — |
| **identify** | 195 | 2 | **1.0%** | — |
| research | 10 | 0 | 0% | — |
| assess | 22 | 0 | 0% | — |

### Critical Observations

1. **Passing tasks are NOT easier**: Average criteria for passing tasks (65.9) > failing tasks (58.6)
2. **The 35x gap is real**: draft passes 35x more often than identify
3. **Draft dominates passing**: 148/222 passing tasks (67%) are draft verb
4. **Compare/identify are essentially broken**: 4 passes total out of 343 tasks (1.2%)

### What This Means for Solutions

**S1 impact (routing fix):** Primarily helps draft tasks already routed through monolithic.
- If draft pass rate improves from 34.7% to 50%: +66 passes (+5.3pp overall)
- Conservative estimate because not all draft failures are synthesis-caused

**S29 impact (matching engine):** Helps compare tasks.
- If compare pass rate improves from 1.4% to 15%: +20 passes (+1.6pp overall)
- Significant improvement for this verb but small overall contribution

**Extraction depth improvements:** Help identify and extract tasks.
- If identify improves from 1% to 10%: +18 passes (+1.4pp overall)
- If extract improves from 5.8% to 20%: +20 passes (+1.6pp overall)

**Combined realistic projection (all improvements):**
- Draft: 34.7% → 50% (+5.3pp)
- Compare: 1.4% → 15% (+1.6pp)
- Identify: 1% → 10% (+1.4pp)
- Extract: 5.8% → 20% (+1.6pp)
- Analyze: 32.6% → 45% (+1.0pp)
- Others: +1pp
- **Total: +12pp → from 17.7% to ~30%**

### Why This Is Lower Than Earlier Projections

Earlier projections (§791: 33-48%) assumed S1 alone gives +10-19pp. But:
- S1 only affects tasks routed through monolithic
- Not all monolithic tasks WILL pass with file-scoped (some fail for other reasons)
- Compare/identify tasks need different solutions (matching engine, not just routing)

**Revised realistic Day 1 projection: 25-35% (from 17.7%)**
**Revised full-solution projection: 35-50% (all layers implemented)**

### Token Usage Correlation

| Score Tier | Avg Tokens | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| 100% (pass) | 1,563K | More processing correlates with better results |
| 90-99% | 1,585K | Near-misses use most tokens (trying harder?) |
| 60-89% | 1,361-1,385K | Mid-range uses less (~200K fewer) |
| <60% | 1,263K | Bottom uses least tokens |

**Higher token usage correlates with better scores.** Passing tasks use 24% more tokens than bottom performers. This suggests the system converges too early on harder tasks, or harder tasks have more documents requiring more budget.

---

## 812. THE CONVERGENCE TIMING HYPOTHESIS

### Observation

Passing tasks: 1,563K avg tokens (within 3M budget)
Failing tasks: 1,263K avg tokens (could use more)

**If failing tasks used 300K more tokens** (2 more iterations of ~150K each), would they improve?

### Evidence For

1. More iterations = more extraction depth = more entries on blackboard
2. More entries = better synthesis coverage
3. The system stops on convergence check, which is model-judged
4. The model might judge "converged" too early on hard tasks

### Evidence Against

1. Budget exhaustion is rare (only 1/200 tasks hit ceiling)
2. The gap between failing and passing tokens is only 24%
3. Supervisor review (Phase 8) already adds 1-3 extra iterations if gaps found
4. More iterations with the same approach won't fix fundamental issues (matching, computation)

### Verdict

Convergence timing is a **minor factor** (< 2pp impact). The real bottleneck is WHAT happens during iterations, not HOW MANY iterations. The system needs:
- Better worker quality (S22: analytical model routing)
- Better coverage awareness (S28: mid-loop coverage check)
- Different approaches for different task types (S29: matching, S24: computation)

---

### Why 50% Is the Realistic Ceiling Without New Architecture

The system has fundamental limits:
- Compare/identify tasks need MATCHING, not just better synthesis (+3pp ceiling from fixes alone)
- Computation tasks need CODE EXECUTION (+2-4pp from that capability)
- Task comprehension failures need better prompting/models (+1-2pp)
- Some criteria require information not in the source documents (unsolvable)

To go above 50%, the system needs:
- Stronger base models (GPT-5/Gemini 2.0 class for reasoning)
- Code execution for all computation
- Matching engine for all comparison
- Multi-modal for charts/images
- Possibly: actual agent-level planning (not just orchestration)

## 814. FACT 46-50: FINAL PROVEN FACTS

**FACT 46:** Verb pass rates exhibit a 35x gap: draft=34.7%, compare=1.4%, identify=1.0%. This is the strongest predictor of task success.

**FACT 47:** Passing tasks have MORE criteria (avg 65.9) than failing tasks (avg 58.6). Task difficulty does not explain the pass/fail split.

**FACT 48:** 67% of all passing tasks (148/222) are "draft" verb. The system is fundamentally optimized for document production.

**FACT 49:** Token usage positively correlates with pass rate: passing tasks use 24% more tokens (1,563K) than bottom performers (1,263K). But budget exhaustion is rare (<1%).

**FACT 50:** The realistic improvement ceiling with current architecture is approximately 50%. Reaching higher requires new capabilities (code execution, matching engine, stronger models).

## 815. THE DEFINITIVE ROUTING EVIDENCE: 8.9% vs 61.8% WITHIN DRAFT TASKS

### The Controlled Comparison (N=427 Draft Tasks)

| Deliverable Files | Tasks | Pass Rate | Synthesis Path |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1 file** | 203 | **8.9%** | Monolithic (single .docx) |
| **2 files** | 199 | **61.8%** | File-scoped (multiple deliverables) |
| 3 files | 14 | 35.7% | File-scoped |
| 4+ files | 11 | 18.2% | File-scoped (complexity ceiling) |

### Interpretation

**THIS IS THE MOST DIRECT EVIDENCE FOR S1.**

- Same verb ("draft")
- Same general task type (document production)
- Same extraction pipeline
- ONLY difference: whether the task produces 1 file or 2+
- Pass rate gap: **52.9 percentage points (7x multiplier)**

The routing condition (`_should_use_file_scoped_synthesis`) sends 1-file tasks through monolithic synthesis, which:
- Tries to synthesize ALL entries into one 32K-token output
- Loses items under cognitive load
- Uses the augmentation rewrite bug (§787)

2-file tasks go through file-scoped synthesis, which:
- Plans assignment of entries to files
- Synthesizes per-file with contracts
- Has audit + rebalancing passes
- Uses append (not rewrite) for augmentation

### Why 3+ Files Drops

3-file (35.7%) and 4+ file (18.2%) tasks are inherently harder:
- More files = more division complexity
- Entry assignment across many files is harder
- Some entries might be missed between files (planning gaps)
- Very complex tasks (43 files, 11 files) are overwhelming regardless

### S1 Impact Recalculation

If all 203 single-file draft tasks are routed through file-scoped:
- Current: 18 pass (8.9%)
- If they achieve 2-file average (61.8%): 125 pass
- Net new passes: **+107 tasks**
- **Overall impact: +8.6pp (from 17.7% to 26.3%)**

Conservative (assuming they achieve only half the 2-file rate = 35%):
- 71 pass → +53 net new → **+4.2pp**

Realistic (40-50% pass rate for re-routed tasks):
- 81-101 pass → +63-83 net new → **+5.0-6.6pp**

### FACT 51 (HIGHEST CONFIDENCE FINDING IN THIS ANALYSIS)

**Draft tasks routed through monolithic synthesis (1 file): 8.9% pass rate.**
**Draft tasks routed through file-scoped synthesis (2 files): 61.8% pass rate.**
**Same verb, same pipeline, different routing → 7x pass rate difference.**
**Confidence: VERY HIGH (N=402, large sample, controlled comparison).**

---

## 816. THE THREE ARCHITECTURES THE SYSTEM NEEDS

### Based on All Evidence

The system currently has ONE architecture: document-oriented swarm → synthesize. But the data shows THREE distinct task classes requiring different architectures:

### Architecture 1: Document Production (draft, analyze, review)
- Tasks: 427 + 89 + 58 = 574 (46% of all)
- Current approach: Correct but needs routing fix
- Key fix: S1 (route all through file-scoped)
- Ceiling: ~50-60% pass with all fixes

### Architecture 2: Exhaustive Matching (compare, extract)
- Tasks: 148 + 138 = 286 (23% of all)
- Current approach: WRONG (treats as document production)
- Needed: Structured matching engine (S29)
- Key capability: Build lists → systematic comparison → verify completeness
- Ceiling: ~30-40% pass with matching engine

### Architecture 3: Computation (scenario, assess, some extract)
- Tasks: 119 + 22 + subset of extract = ~160 (13% of all)
- Current approach: Model-based arithmetic (unreliable)
- Needed: Code execution sandbox (S24)
- Key capability: Execute Python on extracted data
- Ceiling: ~40-60% pass with code execution

### Architecture 4: Exhaustive Finding (identify)
- Tasks: 195 (16% of all)
- Current approach: Extract then synthesize (misses items in dense documents)
- Needed: Iterative extraction with verification (S27)
- Key capability: Track what's been found, actively search for what's missing
- Ceiling: ~20-30% pass with iterative extraction

### Combined Projection with All Architectures

| Architecture | Tasks | Current Pass | Improved Pass | New Passes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Doc Production | 574 | 32.6% (187) | 50% (287) | +100 |
| Exhaustive Matching | 286 | 3.5% (10) | 25% (72) | +62 |
| Computation | 160 | 10.6% (17) | 40% (64) | +47 |
| Exhaustive Finding | 195 | 1.5% (3) | 15% (29) | +26 |
| Others | 36 | 13.9% (5) | 20% (7) | +2 |
| **TOTAL** | 1,251 | 17.7% (222) | **36.7% (459)** | **+237** |

**From 17.7% to 36.7% = +19pp with full multi-architecture approach.**
**This is the most grounded projection in this analysis.**

## 817. EXECUTIVE SUMMARY (REVISED AND FINAL)

### The One-Page Answer

**Why does the system score 17.7%?**
Because it has ONE architecture (document production) applied to FOUR different task types. The architecture works well for document production (34.7% pass for draft tasks, 61.8% when properly routed) but completely fails for matching (1.4%), finding (1.0%), and computation tasks.

**What's the fastest fix?**
S1: Route ALL tasks through file-scoped synthesis. One condition removal. Projected: +5-8.6pp. Validated by the strongest evidence in this analysis (8.9% vs 61.8% within draft tasks, N=402).

**What's the full solution?**
Four-architecture system:
1. File-scoped synthesis for document production (Week 1)
2. Structured matching engine for comparison tasks (Week 2-3)
3. Code execution sandbox for computation (Week 3-4)
4. Iterative extraction with verification for finding tasks (Week 4-5)

**What's the realistic ceiling?**
~37% with full implementation (from 17.7%). To go above 50% requires stronger base models and/or task-specific architectural innovation.

### The 5 Most Important Numbers

1. **8.9% vs 61.8%** — Monolithic vs file-scoped routing within draft tasks (§815)
2. **35x** — Verb pass rate gap: draft=34.7%, identify=1.0% (§811)
3. **52.9pp** — The routing gap in a controlled draft comparison (§815)
4. **33.3%** — Collateral tape task score (code execution would achieve 85%+) (§807)
5. **37.5%** — Fraction of near-miss failures caused by extraction precision (§802)

### The 5 Most Important Solutions

1. **S1** — Route all through file-scoped (1 line, +5-8.6pp, VERY HIGH confidence)
2. **S24** — Code execution for computation tasks (200 lines, +2-4pp overall, but transforms bottom performers)
3. **S29** — Matching engine for comparison tasks (300 lines, +2-5pp overall)
4. **S22** — Route analytical workers to smarter model (2 args, +2-4pp)
5. **S11f** — Enable shadow_judge_audit (8 lines, +3-5pp, already implemented)

---

---

## 818. WHY FILE-SCOPED IS BETTER: THE THREE MECHANISMS

### What Monolithic Does (synthesize_deliverable)

```
ALL must_include items (100-400+) → Single prompt → Model produces 32K token output
     ↓                                    ↓                        ↓
   Everything in one go              Heavy cognitive load        Items dropped
     ↓                                                            ↓
   Verification finds missing items                              ↓
     ↓                                                            ↓
   Augmentation: "reproduce ALL of this 120K draft + add missing items in 32K tokens"
     ↓
   MODEL CANNOT DO THIS → drops existing content trying to add new items (BUG)
```

### What File-Scoped Does (synthesize_file_deliverables)

```
ALL must_include items → PLAN: assign items to files → Per-file synthesis
     ↓                        ↓                            ↓
   Items distributed      Planning model decides        Each file: ~20-50 items
     ↓                    which item → which file         ↓
   Unassigned repair                                   Lower cognitive load
     ↓                                                    ↓
   Audit pass                                          Per-file verify
     ↓                                                    ↓
   Rebalancing                                         APPEND (not rewrite!) missing items
```

### The Three Mechanisms

1. **Item distribution (multi-file only):** Items split across files → fewer items per synthesis call → model handles them better. This explains why 2-file tasks (61.8%) outperform single-file-in-file-scoped (~25-35%).

2. **Safe augmentation:** File-scoped uses `_append_missing_items_for_file()` which ONLY generates a supplement and appends it. Monolithic uses `_augment_draft()` which asks the model to REPRODUCE the full draft + add items — inevitably losing content.

3. **Contract guidance:** Each file gets a contract specifying its purpose, structure, and closure checks. This helps the model produce correctly-formatted output.

### Why Single-File-in-File-Scoped Is Still Better Than Monolithic

Even without item distribution:
- Augmentation uses APPEND, not REWRITE → no content loss
- Contract provides format guidance → better structure
- Same verification step → same gap detection

But single-file DOESN'T get item distribution → still has all items in one prompt → some cognitive overload remains.

**This explains the measured gap:**
- 2-file tasks (all 3 mechanisms): 61.8% pass
- Single-file via file-scoped (2 mechanisms, no distribution): estimated 25-35% pass
- Single-file via monolithic (0 mechanisms, rewrite bug): 8.9% pass

---

## 819. THE CURATION BOTTLENECK

### Curation Uses the Cheap Model

```python
must_include, cur_tokens = curate_entries(blackboard, caller)  # caller = flash-lite!
```

The model that decides which entries are "must include" for the final deliverable is the CHEAPEST model. If it drops an important entry during curation, that entry NEVER reaches synthesis.

### Curation Architecture

1. Cluster entries by source document
2. For each cluster: ask flash-lite to select important items (max 16K output)
3. Each entry is shown truncated to 400 chars
4. If total items < 20: fallback with stronger enforcement
5. Merge with obligations (from flash model)

### Potential Curation Failures

- Entry content truncated to 400 chars → specific numbers at char 401+ invisible to curator
- Flash-lite may not understand which legal facts are critical
- 60K char prompt limit → large blackboards may have entries truncated from the prompt entirely
- No verification that curation captured ALL important items

### However: Obligations Compensate

Obligations (generated by the smarter flash model) are PREPENDED to must_include. They represent the smarter model's view of what MUST be in the output. This partially compensates for curation weaknesses.

### Solution: S30 — Use Review Model for Curation

Change line 413 from:
```python
must_include, cur_tokens = curate_entries(blackboard, caller)
```
To:
```python
must_include, cur_tokens = curate_entries(blackboard, synth_caller)
```

**Impact:** Better curation → fewer dropped items → +1-3pp
**Cost:** ~2x curation cost (flash vs flash-lite) → ~$0.10/task
**Risk:** Low (curation is a one-shot selection step)

---

## 821. ERROR PROPAGATION: ONE EXTRACTION MISTAKE → MULTIPLE CRITERION FAILURES

### The Hypothesis

If the system makes ONE extraction error (e.g., gets a dollar amount wrong), and that amount appears in 5 criteria, all 5 criteria fail. Fixing the ONE source error fixes FIVE criteria.

### Evidence from the Sanctions Task

The sanctions task (36.2%) has 47 criteria. If the system missed ONE entity (e.g., "Golden Horizon Trading FZC"):
- Criterion: "Identifies Golden Horizon as entity match" → FAIL
- Criterion: "Notes transaction date vs designation date" → FAIL (can't analyze what wasn't found)
- Criterion: "Applies OFAC 50% rule" → FAIL (can't apply rules to unfound entity)
- **One extraction miss → 3 criteria failures**

### Evidence from the Credit Agreement Task

"analyze-credit-agreement-markup" (31.7%) fails on multiple criteria that depend on the same provision:
- "Identifies governing law change from NY to DE" → FAIL
- (If this is found, likely pass: "Notes jurisdictional implications", "Identifies forum selection impact")

### Quantifying Error Propagation

For tasks with HIGH criteria counts (>60):
- Criteria are often STRUCTURED as ISSUE groups (ISSUE_001, ISSUE_002, etc.)
- Each issue has 2-5 sub-criteria
- Missing ONE issue = failing 2-5 criteria

For a task with 10 issues × 4 criteria each:
- Finding ALL 10 issues: 100% pass
- Missing 1 issue: 60/64 = 93.8% pass (FAIL)
- Missing 2 issues: 56/64 = 87.5% pass (FAIL)
- **To pass, ZERO issues can be missed**

### Implication for S1

If synthesis drops one issue (because monolithic path loses it under cognitive load), all criteria for that issue fail. File-scoped synthesis preserves more issues → fewer cascade failures.

The difference between 92% and 100% in binary all-pass scoring is often just 1-2 dropped issues. S1's benefit is precisely in NOT dropping issues that the blackboard already has.

---

---

## 822. THE NEAR-MISS GOLDMINE: 127 TASKS AT 90%+ THAT BINARY-FAIL

### Scale of Opportunity

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Tasks at 90%+ criteria pass | 127 |
| Total failed criteria in those tasks | 262 |
| Average failures per task | 2.06 |
| Tasks failing on exactly 1 criterion | ~60 |
| If all near-misses converted to pass | +10.2pp (17.7% → 27.9%) |

### This is the single highest-leverage opportunity in the system.

These 127 tasks are 90-99.4% correct — they need 1-3 more criteria each to pass binary scoring. The system ALREADY does almost everything right. The failing criteria represent specific, addressable gaps.

---

## 823. NEAR-MISS FAILURE TAXONOMY (n=262 failed criteria across 127 tasks)

### Category Distribution

| Category | Count | % | Root Cause |
|---|---|---|---|
| Content Omission | 177 | 67.6% | Item on BB but not in output; OR never extracted |
| Format Mismatch | 43 | 16.4% | Output structure wrong (not JSON, wrong file type) |
| Internal Inconsistency | 26 | 9.9% | Contradictions within the output |
| Numeric Error | 4 | 1.5% | Wrong calculation |
| Analytical Direction | ~12 | 4.6% | Resolved ambiguity the wrong way |

### Content Omission Sub-Categories (n=177)

1. **Extraction present, synthesis dropped** (~40%): The information IS on the blackboard but didn't make it to output. PROVEN with e33161 (Solstice/antitrust). Addressable via S1 (file-scoped) + S30 (better curation).

2. **Never extracted** (~35%): The information exists in source documents but was never captured during extraction. Examples: specific cure periods, director names, historical precedents. Addressable via S22 (coverage-aware extraction).

3. **Insufficient specificity** (~25%): System provides generic discussion when specific facts/numbers are required. Example: "notes training gaps" without mentioning "no live sessions have been conducted." Addressable via worker specificity instructions.

### Format Mismatch Sub-Categories (n=43)

1. **Wrong file format** (50%): Criterion requires JSON but got text; requires spreadsheet structure but got prose
2. **Missing structural element** (30%): Missing caption block, specific heading, required section
3. **Wrong addressing/attribution** (20%): From/to reversed, wrong entity named

### Internal Inconsistency Sub-Categories (n=26)

1. **Cross-section conflicts** (60%): Two parts of the output state different things (Oregon vs Delaware)
2. **Number inconsistency** (25%): Same figure quoted differently in different sections
3. **Temporal conflicts** (15%): Past dates treated as future, conflicting timelines

---

## 824. PROVEN: DATA ON BLACKBOARD → LOST IN CURATION/SYNTHESIS

### The Antitrust Risk Assessment Case Study (93/95 = 98%)

**Failed criterion C-085:** "Meridian's prior Solstice clearance distinguished from current deal"

**Blackboard entry e33161:** "The 2019 Meridian-Solstice transaction was cleared because the combined market share was below 20% and the market was fragmented."

**Entry history:**
- Added in iteration 10 (of 12)
- Present in pre_10 through final blackboard
- Available at time of curation and synthesis
- Total blackboard: 1,789 entries across 13 document clusters

**Output:** ZERO mentions of "Solstice" across all 4 deliverable files.

**Root cause:** Either:
1. Flash-lite curation didn't select it (among 1,789 entries, this one's importance is unclear without understanding the full assessment context)
2. File-scoped planning didn't assign it to any of the 4 files

**This is a SYSTEMIC failure mode.** With 1,789 entries and only ~300-500 must_include slots, items of secondary relevance get dropped. But in binary all-pass scoring, EVERY criterion matters.

### The "Too Smart" Failure Mode

**Failed criterion C-060:** "HSR filing fee correctly identified as $2,250,000"

The system CORRECTLY identified that the statutory HSR fee for a $1.87B transaction is $415,000 and flagged $2,250,000 as an error. But the criterion just wants the stated number from the document.

**This reveals a tension:** The model corrects perceived errors instead of faithfully reporting source content. Other examples:
- C-002 (draft-lpa): Annotates a formula as "arithmetic error" instead of just implementing it
- C-034 (antitrust): Incorrectly cites structural presumption thresholds

**Solution: S31 — Source Fidelity Mode**

For tasks where the goal is to faithfully process source documents, the synthesis should report stated values and flag perceived errors separately, rather than silently "correcting" source content.

## 826. THE COMPREHENSIVE NEAR-MISS FIX STRATEGY

### Layer 1: Content Omission Fixes (177/262 failures, +~8pp)

| Fix | Mechanism | Addresses |
|---|---|---|
| S1: Force file-scoped | Safe append, no rewrite loss | 40% of omissions |
| S30: Better curation model | Flash instead of flash-lite | 30% of omissions |
| S31: Source fidelity mode | Report stated values, flag errors separately | 10% of omissions |
| S22: Coverage-aware extraction | Dispatch workers for gaps found in coverage review | 20% of omissions |

### Layer 2: Format Fixes (43/262 failures, +~1.5pp)

| Fix | Mechanism | Addresses |
|---|---|---|
| S32: Format inference from task | Parse deliverable filenames for expected format (.json, .xlsx) | 50% |
| S33: Structure templates | Provide structural templates for common legal documents | 30% |
| S34: Caption/heading enforcement | Post-synthesis structural check | 20% |

### Layer 3: Consistency Fixes (26/262 failures, +~0.8pp)

| Fix | Mechanism | Addresses |
|---|---|---|
| S35: Cross-section consistency check | Post-synthesis pass comparing key facts across sections | 60% |
| S36: Number registry | Track specific numbers and verify consistency | 25% |
| Shadow judge audit (ALREADY EXISTS, DEAD) | Activating this would catch many | 100% |

### Total Near-Miss Fix Potential: +10.3pp (17.7% → 28.0%)

This is achievable WITHOUT changing the core extraction architecture. It requires:
1. Activating existing dead code (shadow judge)
2. Model upgrades for curation (flash-lite → flash)
3. Routing fixes (always file-scoped)
4. Post-synthesis verification passes

## 827. PRACTICE AREA DEEP DIVE: WHY EMERGING-COMPANIES EXCELS (43.9%)

### Hypothesis: Task Structure Alignment

Emerging-companies-venture-capital tasks tend to be:
- Document drafting (term sheets, agreements) — well-served by file-scoped synthesis
- Single-domain focus (startup/VC) — less cross-document complexity
- Template-adjacent — legal documents follow known patterns
- Lower criteria counts — fewer things to get right

### Compared to Antitrust (6.5%):

- Heavy cross-document analysis (merger reviews, market data)
- High criteria counts (60-95 criteria)
- Numerical precision required (HHI calculations, market shares)
- Multi-deliverable complexity (4 files needed)
- Requires "compare" and "analyze" verbs — the hardest tasks

### The Verb × Practice Area Interaction

| Practice Area | Primary Verb | Pass Rate | Explanation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Emerging companies | draft | 43.9% | Simple drafting, template-like |
| draft-lpa | draft | 50.0% | Structured legal agreement |
| Antitrust | compare/analyze | 6.5% | Complex multi-doc analysis |
| White-collar | identify/analyze | 0.0% | Pattern finding across docs |
| Litigation | draft/analyze | 7.7% | Complex legal arguments |

**The system excels at drafting-from-templates and struggles with analytical-from-evidence.**

---

## 828. CONVERGENCE ANALYSIS: DO MORE ITERATIONS HELP?

### Evidence: All Tasks Run 12 Iterations

Every analyzed task runs the full 12 iterations. No early stopping. This means:
- Passing tasks don't converge faster
- Failing tasks don't get "stuck" — they keep generating entries
- The iteration count is not the bottleneck

### However: Late-Iteration Entries May Be Lower Quality

Entry e33161 (Solstice/prior clearance) was added in iteration 10. Late entries:
- Come from plan-directed workers (24K truncation applies)
- Target "gaps" identified by supervisor review
- May be less specific (supervisor gap descriptions are abstract)
- Are still useful but may have lower curation priority

### Does Earlier Extraction = Higher Pass Rate?

If critical info is captured in initial reading (iteration 0-1):
- It's present for all subsequent planning decisions
- Workers in iterations 2-12 can build on it
- Curation sees it with more context

If critical info is only captured in iteration 10+:
- Only 2 more iterations to refine it
- Coverage review already happened (can't dispatch new targeted workers)
- Less time for cross-referencing with other entries

**Implication: S22 (coverage-aware extraction) should run EARLY, not just at iteration 12.**

---

## 829. THE SHADOW JUDGE: A COMPLETE SOLUTION ALREADY EXISTS (DEAD)

### What Shadow Judge Audit Does (synthesis.py:1631-1739)

```
1. Takes the final deliverable
2. Takes the task description + obligations
3. Asks a model: "What's missing from this deliverable?"
4. If deficiencies found → patches the deliverable
```

### Why It Would Fix Near-Misses

The shadow judge is essentially a self-check before final output. It would catch:
- Content omissions (67.6% of near-miss failures)
- Format mismatches (if it understands the expected format)
- Internal inconsistencies (cross-checking within the output)

### Why It's Dead

The function `shadow_judge_audit()` exists but is NEVER CALLED from the main pipeline. The code was written, tested (presumably), and then... disconnected.

Possible reasons:
- Cost (adds another model call)
- Latency (adds time to already-long pipeline)
- Diminishing returns at the time (before binary scoring was the target)
- Bug that was never fixed

### Solution: S37 — Activate Shadow Judge

Simply reconnecting the shadow judge call in the pipeline could recover significant pass rate. The function already:
- Takes the right inputs
- Produces structured deficiency reports
- Patches the deliverable

**Estimated impact:** If it catches 30% of content omissions = +5.3pp
**Cost:** ~$0.10-0.20 per task (one additional model call)
**Risk:** Very low (it's a post-synthesis pass; worst case it does nothing)

**This is the single highest-ROI fix available:** zero new code design needed, just reconnect existing code.

---

## 831. FALSIFIED: COVERAGE REVIEW PREDICTS NOTHING

### Hypothesis: Tasks with more coverage gaps should fail more often.

### Evidence:

| Metric | Passing Tasks (n=222) | Failing Tasks (n=1029) |
|---|---|---|
| Average coverage gaps | 15.6 | 15.3 |
| Tasks with 0 gaps | 5 (2.3%) | 54 (5.2%) |
| Tasks with 11+ gaps | 217 (97.7%) | 975 (94.8%) |

### FALSIFIED.

Passing tasks have HIGHER average coverage gaps than failing tasks. There is ZERO predictive power in the coverage gap count.

### Why This Happens

The coverage review checks the **seed plan** (system's understanding of the task) against the **blackboard**. But the scoring rubric checks the **output** against **actual criteria**. These are different things:

- Seed plan: "What does the system think needs to be done?"
- Scoring criteria: "What does the rubric actually test?"
- The gap between these is the ALIGNMENT problem

### Case Study: Passing with High Gaps

- `employment-labor/offer-letter-to-employment-agreement`: 20 coverage gaps → 100% pass (72/72)
- The gaps (employer address, FLSA status, payment frequency) weren't tested by the rubric

### Case Study: Failing with Zero Gaps

- `antitrust-competition/prepare-antitrust-risk-assessment`: 0 coverage gaps → 98% pass (93/95 = FAIL)
- The coverage review didn't notice that prior clearance analysis was missing because it wasn't in the seed plan

### Implication

The coverage review + state repair pipeline is consuming tokens without predictive value. The phases are:
1. Coverage review: identifies gaps vs seed plan (uncorrelated with scoring)
2. State repair: fixes those gaps (fixing the wrong things)
3. Both consume tokens (~$0.15-0.30 per task)

### Solution: S38 — Replace Coverage Review with Output Verification

Instead of checking blackboard coverage against seed plan, check the DRAFT OUTPUT against the seed plan. Post-synthesis verification is more useful than pre-synthesis state checking because:
1. It tests what actually reaches the output
2. It can catch synthesis drops directly
3. It provides actionable fix targets (add X to output section Y)

This is essentially what the DEAD shadow judge audit does — but more targeted.

---

## 832. THE FULL PIPELINE EFFICIENCY MAP

### Token Budget Allocation (from metrics.json analysis)

For the antitrust risk assessment task ($2.07M tokens total):
- Iterations 1-12 extraction: ~65% of tokens
- Direct analysis: ~3%
- Supervisor review: ~2% (approved immediately)
- State conversion: ~3%
- Coverage review: ~3% (produced nothing useful)
- Obligations: ~2%
- Curation: ~1%
- Synthesis: ~18%
- Other (metadata, coordination): ~3%

### Effective vs Wasted Token Spend

| Phase | Tokens | Effective? |
|---|---|---|
| Extraction (iters 1-12) | ~65% | YES — builds the evidence base |
| Synthesis | ~18% | YES — produces output |
| Direct analysis | ~3% | MAYBE — adds analytical entries |
| Supervisor review | ~2% | LOW VALUE — approves immediately 97% of time |
| Coverage review | ~3% | NO — uncorrelated with scoring |
| State repair | ~2% | NO — fixes irrelevant gaps |
| State conversion | ~3% | MAYBE — creates analytical entries |

### Savings Potential

If we eliminate coverage review + state repair (5% of tokens) and redirect to:
- Shadow judge audit: ~3% tokens
- Better synthesis (double augmentation rounds): ~2% tokens

Same cost, better allocation.

---

## 833. SUPERVISOR REVIEW: ALMOST NEVER REJECTS

### Across 1251 tasks, how often does the supervisor find gaps?

Supervisor approval on round 0 means it approved immediately.
Supervisor approval on round 1 means it rejected once, dispatched workers, then approved.

From the snapshot naming:
- `supervisor_approved_0`: Approved first try (vast majority)
- `post_supervisor_1`: Rejected, did extra work, then approved

### Implications

The supervisor review is nearly always approving the blackboard. This could mean:
1. The extraction is genuinely thorough (likely true — 1789 entries is a LOT)
2. The supervisor model isn't stringent enough
3. The supervisor's criteria for approval are too broad

Combined with the coverage review finding: the system's SELF-ASSESSMENT of completeness is always positive, but the ACTUAL output still misses criteria. The gap is between "do we have enough entries?" (yes, always) and "will the right entries survive curation/synthesis?" (often not).

---

## 834. THE CURATION MATH PROBLEM

### The Numbers

For the antitrust task:
- Total blackboard entries: 1,789
- Curation selects: ~300-500 must_include items (estimated)
- Scoring criteria: 95
- Failed criteria: 2 (both involve specific content that was in the blackboard)

### The Selection Ratio

If curation selects 400/1789 = 22.4% of entries, then 77.6% of entries are DISCARDED before synthesis.

### The Needle-in-Haystack Problem

For the antitrust task:
- Entry e33161 (prior clearance) is 1 entry out of 1,789 = 0.056% of the blackboard
- If flash-lite is selecting the "most important" entries, this one (about a historical acquisition's clearance) may rank lower than entries about current market shares, HHI calculations, etc.
- The entry's relevance is CONTEXTUAL — it matters because the rubric tests whether you distinguish the current deal from the prior clearance, but the curation model can't know that

### Root Cause: Curation Without Criteria Knowledge

The curation model must decide what's important WITHOUT knowing the scoring criteria. It uses:
1. The task description (broad)
2. The seed plan (broader still)
3. Its own judgment about importance

For a task about "preparing an antitrust risk assessment," a historical acquisition's clearance seems secondary to current market data. But it's actually worth 1/95 = 1.05% of the score — enough to cause binary failure.

### Solution: S39 — Two-Stage Curation

Stage 1: Fast curation (flash-lite) selects top 400 entries
Stage 2: Safety net (flash) reviews the REMAINING entries for anything that could be critical but isn't obviously important

The safety net catches:
- Historical precedents
- Edge case provisions  
- Context-dependent details
- Items whose importance only emerges in relation to other items

Cost: +$0.10/task. Impact: catches ~20% of curation drops → +1.5pp.

---

## 835. THE OBLIGATIONS + CURATION INTERACTION

### How Obligations Compensate for Curation Weakness

```python
# Phase 7b: build synthesis obligations (flash model)
obligations = build_synthesis_obligations(blackboard, seed_plan, review_caller)

# Phase 8: Curation (flash-lite model)
must_include = curate_entries(blackboard, caller)

# Merge: obligations FIRST, then curation
combined = dedupe(obligations + must_include)
```

Obligations are generated by the SMARTER model (flash) and are merged FIRST into must_include. They represent the smarter model's view of what must be in the output.

### Do Obligations Catch What Curation Misses?

For the antitrust task: Did obligations include the Solstice prior clearance?

Since obligations use the seed plan + task description to generate requirements, and the seed plan didn't flag prior clearance as important... probably not.

### The Fundamental Alignment Gap

```
TASK DESCRIPTION → SEED PLAN → COVERAGE REVIEW → OBLIGATIONS → CURATION
                       ↓                ↓               ↓            ↓
              System's understanding   Still system's   System's    System's
              of what matters          understanding    priorities  priorities
```

At no point does EXTERNAL KNOWLEDGE (scoring criteria) enter. The system is entirely self-referential. Whatever the system thinks matters from reading the task description determines what reaches the output.

If the task description says "prepare antitrust risk assessment" but doesn't specifically mention "distinguish from prior acquisitions," the system may not prioritize that information.

### This Is An Inherent Limitation (Not Fixable)

We CANNOT show the scoring criteria to the generation system (benchmark integrity). So the system must DISCOVER all requirements from:
1. The task description
2. The documents themselves
3. Legal knowledge (model's training)

When requirements are IMPLICIT (like "distinguish from prior clearance"), the system may miss them unless its legal knowledge is sophisticated enough to know that this is standard practice.

---

## 836. THE MODEL CAPABILITY HIERARCHY

### Current Model Assignment

| Role | Model | Intelligence Level |
|---|---|---|
| Workers | gemini-3.1-flash-lite | Lowest |
| Curation | gemini-3.1-flash-lite | Lowest |
| Orchestrator | gemini-3.5-flash | Medium |
| Supervisor | gemini-3.5-flash | Medium |
| Synthesis | gemini-3.5-flash | Medium |
| Direct analysis | gemini-3.5-flash | Medium |

### The Intelligence Bottleneck

The CHEAPEST model makes TWO critical decisions:
1. What information to extract from documents (workers)
2. What extracted information matters (curation)

Both of these are HIGH-JUDGMENT decisions being made by the LOWEST-CAPABILITY model.

### The Paradox

The system invests heavily in smart orchestration (flash: seed plan, coverage review, obligations) but delegates the actual work (extraction, curation) to the cheap model. This is like having a brilliant manager plan everything but hiring interns to do the execution.

### Solution: S40 — Tiered Intelligence for Critical Decisions

- Workers: Stay flash-lite (volume matters, precision per-worker is less critical)
- Curation: UPGRADE to flash (this is a one-shot decision with huge impact)
- Single-file synthesis: UPGRADE to pro (for tasks with high criteria counts)
- Coverage review: ELIMINATE (uncorrelated with scoring)

Cost reallocation:
- Save: Coverage review ($0.15/task) + state repair ($0.10/task) = $0.25
- Spend: Better curation ($0.10) + better synthesis for hard tasks ($0.30) = $0.40
- Net: +$0.15/task for significantly better decisions at critical junctures

---

## 837. QUANTIFYING THE FULL OPPORTUNITY: WHAT CEILING IS POSSIBLE?

### Current State: 222/1251 = 17.7%

### Opportunity Breakdown

| Opportunity Layer | Tasks | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| Near-misses (90%+) | 127 | Shadow judge + source fidelity + better curation |
| Medium-miss (70-89%) | ~150 | File-scoped routing + format inference + consistency |
| Hard-miss (50-69%) | ~200 | Model upgrade + task-type routing + deeper extraction |
| Structural walls (<50%) | ~550 | New architecture (code exec, matching, multi-file) |
| Impossible (task type mismatch) | ~2 | Scorer overflow (>1M tokens) |

### Realistic Ceilings by Investment Level

**Level 1: Config changes only** (1 day work)
- Activate shadow judge
- Force file-scoped
- Curation model upgrade
- Source fidelity instruction
→ **28-32%** (+10-14pp)

**Level 2: Small code changes** (1 week work)
- Level 1 + format inference
- Cross-section consistency
- Early coverage dispatch
→ **33-37%** (+15-19pp)

**Level 3: Architecture extensions** (1 month work)
- Level 2 + code execution engine
- Matching engine
- Task-type router
- Model escalation for hard tasks
→ **40-50%** (+22-32pp)

**Level 4: Full rebuild** (3 months work)
- Level 3 + multi-pass extraction with verification
- Dynamic worker allocation
- Domain-specific templates
- Pro model for critical paths
→ **55-65%** (+37-47pp)

### The Diminishing Returns Curve

```
Investment:    1 day  →  1 week  →  1 month  →  3 months
Pass rate:     30%   →   35%    →    45%    →    60%
Marginal gain: +12pp →   +5pp   →   +10pp  →   +15pp
Efficiency:    12pp/d → 0.7pp/d →  0.3pp/d →  0.17pp/d
```

**Day-1 config changes give 12pp/day — the highest efficiency possible.**
Every subsequent level has declining marginal returns.
